title: Going Further
text:
For many historians, the free tiers of ChatGPT, Google Gemini, and Claude will be sufficient for occasional use: a few transcription sessions, some translation help, the odd coding question. If you use these tools a few times a week for modest tasks, you may never need to pay for anything.
Google’s NotebookLM is also worth exploring: it lets you upload your own sources and query them conversationally, grounding responses in your materials rather than general training data. See the Research Tasks page for a fuller discussion of what it can and cannot do.

title: Going Further
text:
When Free Is Not Enough
You are likely to hit the limits of free tools if you:

Process large volumes of documents. Free tiers have daily usage caps. A sustained transcription or translation project will exhaust them quickly.
Work with long documents. Free models often have smaller context windows — they can only process a limited amount of text at once. If you need to feed in a 30-page chapter or a long set of notes, you may need a paid tier.
Need the most capable models. The best-performing models are typically reserved for paid subscribers. The difference is noticeable for complex tasks like nuanced translation, sophisticated code generation, or working with messy data.
Want to build things. If you want to create databases, process archival images at scale, build a website, or develop custom analysis tools, you will need paid access to agentic tools that can read and write files on your computer.

title: Going Further
text:
The Three Platforms
Prices and capabilities change frequently. The following is accurate as of early 2026 but should be verified before subscribing. All three major platforms now offer comparable pricing (~$20 USD/month), million-token context windows, and built-in web search. The differences lie in their strengths.

Claude Pro (~$20 USD/month)

Access to Anthropic’s Claude models (Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.6)
1 million token context window — Claude can process book-length documents in a single conversation
File uploads, including large PDFs
Careful, detailed responses; tends to be more cautious about stating things it is unsure of
Includes access to Claude Code, Cowork, and Research tools (see below)

Best for: Working with long documents, detailed transcription, and tasks where you value accuracy and caution over speed. Claude’s combination of a very large context window and careful handling of uncertainty makes it a strong default choice for historians. In coding and complex reasoning tasks, Claude currently leads the major benchmarks.


ChatGPT Plus (~$20 USD/month)

Access to OpenAI’s most capable models (currently GPT-5.4)
File uploads (PDFs, images, spreadsheets)
Custom instructions that persist across conversations
Web browsing, image generation, and video generation
The largest user base and ecosystem of guides and tutorials

Best for: General-purpose use across transcription, translation, and writing tasks. ChatGPT remains the most widely used AI tool, and its breadth of features (image generation, computer use, custom GPTs) is unmatched. If you want a single subscription that does a bit of everything, this is the safe choice. OpenAI also offers a budget tier (“Go”) at $8/month for lighter use.


Google AI Pro (~$20 USD/month)

Access to Google’s most capable models (currently Gemini 3 Pro)
1 million token context window
Deep integration with Google Workspace (Docs, Sheets, Gmail)
Deep Research mode for multi-step literature searches
Free for students (one-year Google AI Pro access via .edu email)

Best for: Historians already embedded in the Google ecosystem, and anyone doing exploratory literature reviews or historiographic surveys. Gemini’s Deep Research mode, which executes multi-step web searches and synthesizes results into a structured report, is particularly useful for surveying a field or mapping a historiographic debate. It draws more heavily on Google Scholar than the other platforms do. Be aware, however, that Deep Research still relies primarily on openly available web content and will miss paywalled journals, so it is a starting point for a literature review rather than a substitute for one.

title: Going Further
text:
Beyond the Chatbot: Agentic Tools
The most significant development in 2025-2026 has been the rise of agentic AI tools that go beyond conversation. Instead of answering questions in a chat window, these tools can read your files, write code, execute commands, and complete multi-step tasks on your computer. All three major platforms now offer these.

Claude Code and Cowork
Claude Code runs in your computer’s terminal (command line) and can read, write, and edit files directly. It is included with a Claude Pro subscription. Cowork, launched in early 2026, brings the same file-access capabilities into the Claude desktop app without requiring the terminal.
What historians have used Claude Code for:

Building a professional website in minutes. This guide’s website was built with Claude Code: the Quarto project, the page structure, the CSS styling, and the deployment to GitHub Pages were all created through conversation with the tool.
Creating and managing databases. Loading historical census data into Neo4j, cleaning and restructuring large spreadsheets, building queryable research databases from messy sources.
Processing thousands of archival images. Using the Claude API to send batches of digitized documents (photographs, manuscripts, maps) through automated transcription and description pipelines.
Learning computational methods interactively. Instead of working through a textbook, you can learn text mining, network analysis, or spatial methods through a back-and-forth session where Claude Code writes the code, explains what it does, runs it on your data, and helps you interpret the results.
Creating bespoke visualizations. Generating custom maps, charts, and figures tailored to your specific research questions and data, iterating on the design in real time.
Building local RAG systems. Setting up a system where Claude can search and explore your own research documents (PDFs, transcriptions, notes) locally on your machine, grounding its responses in your specific sources rather than its general training data.

Claude Code is the most powerful option discussed in this guide but also the one with the steepest learning curve. It requires basic comfort with the command line, though Cowork lowers this barrier significantly. For historians doing computationally intensive research, the investment in learning it can pay off substantially.


OpenAI Codex CLI
OpenAI’s Codex CLI is a direct competitor to Claude Code: an open-source terminal tool that reads, modifies, and executes code on your machine. It is included with ChatGPT Plus. It offers configurable approval modes (from read-only to full autonomy) and uses a specialized coding model.


Gemini CLI
Google’s Gemini CLI is the newest entry in this category. It is open-source and free to use with a personal Google account. It includes built-in Google Search, file operations, and support for extensions. A recently added “plan mode” lets it analyze a codebase and design an implementation strategy without modifying any files.


Which agentic tool to choose?
As of early 2026, Claude Code is the most mature and capable of these tools, particularly for complex, multi-step tasks. Codex CLI and Gemini CLI are functional alternatives but newer and less battle-tested. If you are already paying for one platform’s subscription, start with its agentic tool before adding another.

title: Going Further
section: Do You Need Any of This?
text:
Do You Need Any of This?
Honestly — maybe not. If you are writing a traditional monograph based on archival research, and your sources are in languages you read fluently, and you do not work with large datasets, the free tier of any major platform (or NotebookLM) may serve you well for the occasional task. The paid tools are most valuable for researchers whose work involves:

Large-scale document processing
Sources in multiple languages
Computational methods (databases, text analysis, mapping)
Long and complex writing projects
Building custom research infrastructure

Start with the free tools. If you find yourself bumping against their limits regularly, a $20/month subscription is a modest investment for a professional research tool.

title: Going Further
text:
A Note on Privacy
When you use a web-based LLM, the text you provide is sent to the company’s servers. For most research tasks this is not a concern, but be aware:

Do not paste unpublished, embargoed, or confidential material into a free-tier chatbot without understanding the service’s data policy.
Most paid tiers offer options to opt out of having your conversations used for model training. Check the settings.
If you are working with sensitive data (e.g., records involving living individuals), consult your institution’s research ethics guidelines.
Agentic tools like Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI run on your local machine but still send your prompts and file contents to remote servers for processing. The same privacy considerations apply.

title: Research Tasks
text: This section walks through specific research tasks where GenAI tools have proven useful to working historians. Each subsection includes sample prompts you can adapt. The emphasis throughout is on using the tool to accelerate mechanical work while keeping the interpretive judgement in your hands.

title: Research Tasks
text: This section walks through specific research tasks where GenAI tools have proven useful to working historians. Each subsection includes sample prompts you can adapt. The emphasis throughout is on using the tool to accelerate mechanical work while keeping the interpretive judgement in your hands.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Transcription

The Use Case
You have digitized images of manuscripts, printed documents, or typescripts that need to be converted to searchable, editable text. Traditional OCR (optical character recognition) struggles with handwriting, damaged pages, and pre-twentieth-century typefaces. LLMs with vision capabilities (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) can often do better because they use context — they understand that a colonial dispatch is likely to contain certain words and phrases, which helps them read ambiguous characters.


How to Do It

Upload an image of the document page to the chat interface. Most current tools accept image files (JPG, PNG) and PDFs.
Provide context in your prompt:


This is a page from a handwritten letter written by a Hudson’s Bay Company factor at York Factory, circa 1820. Please transcribe the text as accurately as possible. Preserve original spelling, capitalization, and punctuation. Where you are uncertain about a word, mark it with [?].


Review the output against the original image. Correct errors and ask follow-up questions:


In line 7, you transcribed “provisions” — I think the original reads “previsions.” Can you look at that word again?



Tips

Provide as much context as you can: the document type, date, language, and any distinctive features (e.g., “this writer uses the long s”).
Process one page at a time for best results.
For large-scale transcription projects (hundreds of pages), consider using a model’s API rather than the web interface. As Mark Humphries has demonstrated, Gemini 3 via the API achieves character error rates of under 1% on English-language handwritten documents — comparable to expert human typists and dramatically better than specialized HTR tools like Transkribus, at roughly one cent per page.1 The web interface versions of these models often use different (weaker) models than the API, so results may vary depending on how you access the tool.
Note that transcription accuracy varies significantly across models. As of late 2025, Google’s Gemini leads on handwriting recognition, well ahead of Claude and ChatGPT on this specific task. Model capabilities change rapidly, so check current benchmarks if accuracy is critical for your project.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Translation

The Use Case
You are working with primary sources in a language you can read with difficulty, or a language where you need to process many documents quickly. You do not need a publication-quality translation — you need a working draft that gets you close enough to assess the source’s relevance and content.


How to Do It

Please translate the following passage from 18th-century French into English. This is from a colonial administrator’s report on trade in the Antilles. Preserve the meaning as closely as possible; I do not need an elegant literary translation. Flag any terms that are ambiguous or that you are uncertain about.
[paste the French text here]



Tips

Always compare the translation against the original for key passages you plan to cite.
LLMs handle French, Spanish, German, Dutch, and Latin well. Performance degrades for less-represented languages and for heavily abbreviated or formulaic text (e.g., notarial records).
For period-specific vocabulary, tell the model the era: “This is 17th-century legal French; habitant here means settler-farmer, not inhabitant.”
If you plan to quote a translated passage in a publication, have the translation verified independently. LLM translation is a research aid, not a citable source.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Literature Discovery

The Use Case
You are beginning a new project or entering an unfamiliar historiography and want to identify key works, major debates, and starting points for reading.


How to Do It

I am starting a research project on the role of insurance in the British slave trade, roughly 1700–1807. Can you identify the key secondary works I should read? I am particularly interested in the intersection of financial history and the history of slavery.



The Critical Caveat
LLMs frequently hallucinate citations. They generate titles, authors, and publication details that look correct but do not correspond to any real publication. This is one of the most dangerous failure modes for historians.
Rules for using LLM-generated reading lists:

Treat every citation as a suggestion to verify, not a confirmed reference.
Search for each title in your library catalogue, JSTOR, or Google Scholar before adding it to your bibliography.
If a title does not turn up in any database, it probably does not exist.
The model is better at identifying well-known works than obscure articles. Use it for orientation, not for comprehensive literature review.



Tips

Ask for the bibliographic details explicitly: “Please include the full title, author, publisher, and date for each work.”
Cross-reference with review essays, Oxford Bibliographies, or historiographic surveys for the field.
Consider this a complement to, not a replacement for, searching databases yourself.



Google NotebookLM: A Free Research Assistant
Google NotebookLM deserves special attention as a tool for literature discovery and source analysis. Unlike the general-purpose chatbots discussed above, NotebookLM is designed around your sources. You upload documents (PDFs, Google Docs, web pages, YouTube videos) and the tool answers questions grounded in that material, citing specific passages from your uploads rather than drawing on its general training data.
What makes it powerful:

Source-grounded responses. Every answer includes inline citations pointing back to your uploaded documents. This dramatically reduces hallucination compared to asking a general chatbot about your topic.
Audio Overview. NotebookLM can convert your uploaded sources into a podcast-style discussion between two AI voices. This sounds like a gimmick, but historians working through large reading lists have found it genuinely useful: you can listen to a synthesized discussion of a set of articles while commuting or walking, then return to the originals with a better sense of the key arguments.
Free. The free tier is generous: 100 notebooks, 50 sources per notebook, up to 50,000 words per source, and 50 chat queries per day. A paid tier (NotebookLM Plus, included with some Google Workspace plans) raises these limits.
Comparative analysis. You can upload multiple sources and ask NotebookLM to compare arguments, identify points of agreement and disagreement, or trace how a particular concept is treated across different texts. This is especially useful when surveying a historiographic debate.

Where it struggles:

Handwriting and difficult documents. NotebookLM uses lower-tier models to process free-tier content. For relatively clean printed text, OCR and comprehension are strong. For handwritten documents, damaged pages, or complex layouts, results degrade significantly compared to what you would get from the best models (Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Opus) via paid access or the API. Worse, when NotebookLM misreads a handwritten source, it can hallucinate content and then cite the source as evidence for claims the source does not actually contain. Because the tool’s design encourages you to trust its inline citations, this failure mode is particularly insidious: you may click through to the linked source and find that the passage NotebookLM cited does not support (or even contain) the claim it attributed to it. If you push back, the model may double down rather than acknowledge the error. For handwritten or otherwise difficult sources, verify every cited passage against the original.
Source limitations. It can only work with what you upload. It does not search the web or access databases on your behalf. You need to curate the source material yourself.
Depth of analysis. For straightforward questions (“What does Author X argue about Y?”), NotebookLM is excellent. For more interpretive work (“How does Author X’s argument compare to the archival evidence I found in Series Z?”), it is limited by both the quality of the uploaded sources and the capacity of the underlying model.

Best for: Surveying a set of secondary sources you have already collected, preparing for a historiographic essay, digesting a large reading list, or exploring a collection of transcribed primary sources. Think of it as a research assistant who has read everything you have uploaded and can answer questions about it on demand, but who cannot go to the library for you.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Coding and Data Management

The Use Case
You have data — a spreadsheet of census records, a collection of digitized documents, a database of shipping records — and you need to clean, transform, analyse, or visualize it. You are not a programmer, or you are a beginning programmer who gets stuck on syntax.
This is one of the areas where LLMs provide the most dramatic productivity gains for historians. Tasks that might take a week to learn from scratch can often be accomplished in an afternoon with LLM assistance.


How to Do It

I have a CSV file with 428 rows of data about Saskatchewan settlements from the 1921 census. The columns include settlement name, founding year, population, railway company, and year of railway arrival. Some cells contain multiple values separated by semicolons (e.g., a settlement served by two railway companies). I want to write a Python script that:

Reads the CSV file
Splits the multi-value cells into separate rows
Creates a summary table showing, for each railway company, how many settlements it served and the average population of those settlements
Exports the result to a new CSV file

Please write the script and explain each step so I can understand what it does.



Tips

Be specific about your data: describe the columns, the format, any irregularities.
Ask the model to explain the code, not just write it. Understanding what the script does allows you to modify it later and catch errors.
Always test the code on your actual data. The model may make assumptions about your file structure that are not correct.
Start with small, well-defined tasks. Once you are comfortable, you can tackle larger data projects.
If the first attempt does not work, paste the error message back into the conversation. The model is usually good at diagnosing and fixing its own mistakes.



What This Looks Like in Practice
A historian building a database of plantation estates might use an LLM to:

Write a script that scrapes structured data from a website
Clean inconsistent date formats across thousands of records
Generate Cypher queries to load data into a Neo4j graph database
Create visualizations of temporal patterns (e.g., settlement founding dates by decade)

None of this requires the historian to become a software engineer. It requires them to describe clearly what they want and to verify that the output is correct — skills that transfer directly from historical method.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Organizing and Structuring Notes

The Use Case
You have extensive research notes, archival transcriptions, or document summaries and want to organize them by theme, date, or topic.


How to Do It

I have transcribed notes from 30 letters written by colonial administrators in Jamaica between 1815 and 1834. Each note is a paragraph summarizing the letter’s contents with the date and author. I am going to paste all 30 summaries below. Please organize them chronologically and identify the major recurring themes across the correspondence.



Tips

This works best when you have already done the intellectual work of reading and summarizing. The LLM organizes your notes; it does not generate understanding for you.
Useful for conference paper preparation, chapter outlining, or identifying gaps in your research.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
Several GenAI products now offer a “deep research” or “research” mode that goes beyond a simple chatbot response. Instead of answering your question from memory, these tools search the web, read multiple sources, synthesize what they find, and produce a structured report — often with citations and footnotes.
The major options as of early 2026:

Gemini Deep Research (Google) — available with Google AI Pro ($20/month). Searches the web, reads pages, and produces multi-page reports with source links.
ChatGPT Deep Research (OpenAI) — available with ChatGPT Plus and Pro. Similar approach: extended web browsing, synthesis, and structured output.
Perplexity (perplexity.ai) — available in a free tier with limits, and a Pro tier (~$20/month). Designed from the ground up as a research tool. Every claim is linked to a source. The free tier is a good starting point.
Claude with web search (Anthropic) — Claude Pro can search the web when asked. Less automated than the dedicated deep research tools, but the underlying model is strong on synthesis and nuance.

These tools represent a genuine step forward from the basic chatbot experience described in earlier sections. They can produce results in minutes that would take hours of manual searching. They are also where GenAI’s limitations become most consequential for historians.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
Two Cases: Discovery and Fabrication
The best way to understand what deep research tools can and cannot do is to see them in action. The two cases below, both drawn from real research projects, illustrate the full range of outcomes: from genuine historiographic contribution to confident, systematic fabrication. Both outputs looked equally authoritative. Only domain expertise and careful verification could tell the difference.

Case 1: The Algonquin Land Claim — A Genuine Discovery
A historian had spent three years researching the history of Westmeath Township and the Algonquin in the Ottawa Valley. Through conventional archival work, he had assembled a detailed picture of Algonquin petitions and land claims using Joan Holmes & Associates’ comprehensive research reports, which included many transcribed petitions. But a key piece of the story remained elusive: why had the promising 1835 negotiations between Algonquin leaders and Lieutenant-Governor Sir John Colborne collapsed so abruptly in 1836?
When the historian ran a deep research query on the topic, Gemini produced a structured report that identified two sources he had not encountered in three years of research:

Brian Lawson, “Aboriginal Dispossession in the Emergence of a Capitalist Resource Extractive Region: The Ottawa Valley, 1800–1830s” (a conference paper presented at the Canadian Political Science Association, 2009), which analyzed the political economy behind the Crown’s treatment of the Algonquin claims.
A Government of Canada publication from the federal publications archive, containing additional documentation of the negotiations.

These sources provided the missing context: Colborne had left Toronto in late 1835 and been replaced by Sir Francis Bond Head, who was ideologically opposed to creating new reserves near areas of active settlement. Head revoked the agreement Colborne’s administration had made, citing a contested interpretation of the Crawford Purchase (a 1783 treaty with the Mississauga) as justification. This explained the abrupt reversal that the Holmes reports documented but did not fully account for.
The historian followed his standard practice: Interesting, I wonder if that is true. He located the Lawson PDF, read it carefully, and confirmed that it contained the analysis of the Colborne-to-Head transition and its implications for the Algonquin reserve proposal. The source was real, and the key historical argument held up. Not every detail in the deep research report was necessarily correct — for example, some of the narrative about specific meetings may have been embellished or inferred beyond what the sources support — but the tool had surfaced the crucial sources and the essential connection between them. That was enough to answer a question that had remained unresolved for years.
Crucially, this finding had not been flagged by three peer reviewers who had read the historian’s earlier work on the topic. The deep research tool surfaced a connection across a conference paper and a government publication that conventional research methods and expert human review had both missed.


Case 2: Marine Insurance and the Slave Trade — Systematic Fabrication
The same verification habit — Interesting, I wonder if that is true — produced very different results when applied to a deep research report on marine insurance and the British Atlantic slave trade. This report, reproduced in full as a worked example, was generated by Gemini Deep Research in response to a query about the key secondary literature and scholarly debates on the topic. It was fluent, well-structured, and read like the work of a knowledgeable historian. Careful verification revealed systematic errors across several categories.

Distorted quantitative claims
The report cited Pearson and Richardson’s 2019 study in the Journal of Economic History as finding that direct slave voyages accounted for “5 to 10 percent” of total marine insurance premiums and bilateral plantation trade for “~30 percent,” with a combined estimate of “33 percent and 40 percent.” The actual article reports 7 percent for slaving voyages and 41 percent combined — specific, single findings, not the vague ranges the report presented. The report appears to have blended Pearson and Richardson’s numbers with Nicholas Draper’s earlier 33% figure for 1769–1770, then fabricated a “consensus” range of 33–40% that appears nowhere in the literature.
Matthew David Mitchell’s monograph The Prince of Slavers says Humphry Morice trafficked “over 25,000” enslaved Africans. The report inflated this to “over 30,000.”


Garbled and fabricated citations
Mallory Hope’s article was cited as “Underwriting the Slave Trade: Risk, Uncertainty, and Marine Insurance in the Eighteenth-Century French Atlantic.” The actual title is “Risk and Uncertainty in France’s Atlantic Slave Trade” — and the article analyzes the French Atlantic, not the British system. The report presented it as if it were a comparative study of the British insurance market.
The ISBNs for both Ian Baucom’s Specters of the Atlantic and Mitchell’s The Prince of Slavers were wrong — close enough to look plausible, but with transposed or altered digits. This is a hallmark of LLM fabrication: the numbers look right at a glance but do not correspond to any real edition.
The report cited “Lloyd’s Historical Research (Recent)” as a source for the 5–10% / 30% breakdown. This does not correspond to any identifiable publication. It appears to be a wholly invented institutional citation designed to anchor a fabricated data point.


Invented scholarly consensus
Perhaps most dangerously, the report constructed a false consensus by blending figures from different scholars, periods, and methodologies into composite ranges that no individual source supports. It presented these ranges in a table with scholarly attributions, giving the fabricated synthesis the visual authority of a literature review. A reader unfamiliar with the field would have no reason to doubt it.


Mischaracterized arguments
Anita Rupprecht’s article on the Zong massacre was summarized as arguing that “abolitionists deliberately stripped the event of its complex insurance logic.” Rupprecht’s actual argument is more nuanced: she traces how the case was transformed as it circulated through abolitionist propaganda, not that abolitionists conspired to simplify it. The difference matters for how you would use the source.
The “Underwriting Souls” project was attributed solely to Alexandre White at Johns Hopkins, omitting his co-investigator Pyar Seth entirely.



What the Two Cases Tell Us
Both reports were produced by the same tool, using the same technology, with the same confident, authoritative tone. The Algonquin report made a real contribution to an active research project. The insurance report systematically fabricated a scholarly consensus out of distorted numbers, garbled citations, and invented sources. There was nothing in the format, tone, or structure of either report that would allow a reader to distinguish the reliable one from the unreliable one without independent verification.
This is the fundamental challenge of deep research tools for historians: they are most impressive when they are right, and most dangerous when they are wrong, and they look exactly the same in both cases.
The only safeguard is the question the historian applied to both reports: Interesting, I wonder if that is true. In the Algonquin case, verification confirmed a genuine discovery. In the insurance case, verification exposed systematic fabrication. The habit is the same; only the outcome differs.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
What They Do Well

Rapid Orientation in an Unfamiliar Field
If you are beginning a project in a historiography you do not know, a deep research tool can produce a useful map of the terrain in fifteen minutes. Ask:

I am a historian beginning a research project on the role of marine insurance in the British Atlantic slave trade, roughly 1700–1807. Please search for the key secondary literature and major scholarly debates. I am particularly interested in work published since 2000, particularly at the intersection of financial history and the history of slavery. Provide full bibliographic details for each source and a brief summary of its argument.

The result will typically be a structured report identifying major scholars, key monographs and articles, and the main lines of debate. This is genuinely useful as a starting point — comparable to asking a knowledgeable colleague for reading suggestions, but faster and more systematic.


Surfacing Sources Across Disciplines and Archives
As the Algonquin case demonstrates, deep research tools search broadly and can surface relevant work in adjacent fields, government publications, conference papers, and other sources that a historian searching within disciplinary databases might miss. This is a real advantage, particularly for topics that span multiple archives, jurisdictions, or scholarly communities.


Finding Recent Work
Deep research tools that search the web can surface recent publications that may not yet appear in standard bibliographic databases. For fast-moving fields or recent special issues, this is a real advantage over traditional database searching.


Producing Working Bibliographies
The best deep research reports include enough bibliographic detail that you can quickly verify each source in your library catalogue or JSTOR. This makes them useful as a triage tool: generate a long list, verify what exists, and then decide what to read.

title: Deep Research Tools
section: Where They Fail — and Why It Matters More Here
text:
Where They Fail — and Why It Matters More Here
The limitations described in the Limitations section apply to deep research tools with particular force, because the output looks more authoritative. A chatbot response that gets something wrong is easy to spot — it reads like a casual answer. A deep research report with footnotes, structured sections, and scholarly language is much harder to evaluate critically. The very features that make these tools useful also make their failures more dangerous.

Mixed Real and Fabricated Citations
As the insurance case demonstrates, deep research reports typically contain a mixture of:

Real sources, correctly cited and accurately summarized.
Real sources, cited but with wrong titles, wrong ISBNs, or inaccurately summarized.
Fabricated sources that do not exist, presented with plausible-sounding institutional attributions.

The fabricated sources are not flagged. They appear in the same footnote format as the real ones. The ratio has improved — current models fabricate less frequently than their 2024 predecessors — but this makes the problem harder, not easier. When most citations check out, the temptation to stop verifying is strong. The one fabrication you miss is the one that ends up in your footnotes.
This means every single citation in a deep research report must be independently verified. This is not a suggestion; it is a requirement.


Confident Mischaracterization
Even when a source is real, the tool may misrepresent its argument. The insurance report’s treatment of Rupprecht’s article is a clear example: a nuanced argument about how the Zong case was transformed through circulation was flattened into a claim about deliberate simplification by abolitionists. A book that is primarily about one topic may be cited for a claim it makes only in passing. The tool does not distinguish between a work’s central thesis and an incidental observation.


Fabricated Consensus
The most insidious failure mode, visible in the insurance case, is the construction of false scholarly consensus. The tool blended figures from different scholars, different periods, and different methodologies into composite ranges, then presented these ranges in a table with scholarly attributions. The result looked like a literature review summarizing established findings. It was actually a fabrication built from distorted parts of real scholarship. This kind of error is far harder to catch than a simple invented citation, because each individual piece has a kernel of truth.


Source Quality Blindness
These tools do not distinguish between a peer-reviewed article in Past & Present and a blog post. They weight sources by accessibility and relevance to the query, not by scholarly authority. A deep research report may cite high-quality scholarship alongside popular histories, journalism, or Wikipedia summaries without distinguishing between them.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
A Practical Workflow
Given these strengths and limitations, here is a workflow that gets the most value from deep research tools while managing the risks:

Step 1: Generate
Run a deep research query on your topic. Be specific about what you want:

Search for peer-reviewed scholarship published since 2010 on [your topic]. Focus on [specific aspects]. Provide full bibliographic details (author, title, journal/publisher, date, volume, pages) for each source.



Step 2: Verify Every Citation
Go through the report’s citations one by one:

Search for each title in your library catalogue, Google Scholar, JSTOR, or WorldCat.
If a source does not appear in any database, it probably does not exist. Remove it.
If a source exists, check that the author, date, and publication details match.
Check ISBNs. LLMs frequently generate plausible-looking but incorrect ISBNs with transposed digits.

This step is non-negotiable. Budget time for it.


Step 3: Check Summaries Against Originals
For sources you plan to use, read at least the abstract and introduction yourself. Verify that the tool’s summary accurately reflects the work’s argument. Do not rely on the tool’s characterization. As the insurance case shows, even real sources can be subtly misrepresented in ways that distort your understanding of the field.


Step 4: Check the Numbers
If the report presents quantitative claims attributed to specific scholars, verify them against the original sources. The insurance case demonstrated how a tool can take real numbers from real scholars and blend them into fabricated consensus ranges. A table with scholarly attributions is not the same as a table that accurately reflects what those scholars actually found.


Step 5: Fill the Gaps
Deep research reports are biased toward digitally accessible, English-language sources. After verifying what the tool found, search manually for:

Work in other languages relevant to your topic.
Older foundational texts that may not be well-represented online.
Archival sources and primary source collections (which these tools cannot access).
Recent work that may not yet be indexed.



Step 6: Read
There is no substitute for this. The deep research report tells you what might be worth reading. The reading itself is your job.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
Comparing the Tools
Each deep research tool has different strengths. Based on early 2026 capabilities (these change rapidly — verify before relying on this comparison):








Tool
Strengths
Weaknesses




Perplexity
Source links for every claim; free tier available; good for quick factual queries
Can be shallow on historiographic nuance; free tier has limits


Gemini Deep Research
Excellent on well-documented historiography; thorough multi-page reports; strong Google Scholar integration
Fabricates confidently at the edges of knowledge; does not signal uncertainty


ChatGPT Deep Research
Strong synthesis; good at identifying scholarly debates
Citation accuracy varies; can hallucinate convincingly


Claude (web search)
More cautious; better at flagging uncertainty and acknowledging gaps
Less automated; requires more guidance



These assessments will change as the tools improve. The verification workflow remains the same regardless of which tool you use.

A Note on Gemini and the Limits of Knowledge
Gemini Deep Research deserves special mention because it illustrates a pattern that applies to all these tools but is particularly visible here. When you ask Gemini about a well-established historiographic debate — the Williams thesis, the standard of living debate, the “great divergence” — it is genuinely impressive. It identifies the key scholars, maps the lines of argument, and produces reports that a graduate student would find useful. As Cameron Blevins tested in late 2025, its synthesis of American Western history was “remarkably accurate.”1
The problem emerges when you push toward questions at the frontier of historical knowledge — topics where the primary sources are thin, not digitized, or simply do not exist. When Gemini cannot find strong evidence, it does not say so. Instead, it answers the question anyway, drawing on whatever it can find: sources from the wrong decade, tangentially related evidence, or speculative claims from weak secondary sources presented with the same confidence as established scholarship. The report reads just as polished and authoritative as one about a well-documented topic. The difference is invisible unless you already know enough to spot it.
This is the fundamental trap of deep research tools: they are most useful when you know the least about a topic, and least reliable in exactly that situation. When you are entering an unfamiliar field — the very moment you most need guidance — you are also least equipped to evaluate what the tool gives you. The verification workflow described above is the only safeguard.

title: Deep Research Tools
section: When Deep Research Tools Are Most Useful
text:
When Deep Research Tools Are Most Useful

Entering a new field. You are starting a project outside your usual area and need a map of the literature.
Checking for recent work. You have not searched a particular topic in a year or two and want to see what has been published.
Interdisciplinary questions. Your project touches on economics, law, environmental science, or another field, and you want to find relevant work outside history journals.
Connecting sources across archives. As the Algonquin case shows, these tools can sometimes link government publications, conference papers, and archival materials that conventional database searches treat as separate silos.
Generating leads, not conclusions. You treat the output as a list of things to check, not as a finished literature review.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
When They Are Not Useful

Your own specialty. You almost certainly know the literature better than the tool does. It may surface the occasional overlooked source — as in the Algonquin case — but the risk of fabricated or mischaracterized sources means you must verify everything regardless.
Archival research. These tools cannot access unpublished sources, manuscript collections, or finding aids.
Historiographic argument. A literature review in a thesis or article must reflect your reading and your interpretation of the field. A deep research report is a starting point for that work, not a substitute for it.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
See It for Yourself
To illustrate what deep research output actually looks like — and why verification is essential — we ran the marine insurance prompt from the examples above through Gemini Deep Research and reproduced the full output as a worked example. The report is impressive, well-structured, and reads like the work of a knowledgeable historian. The detailed verification above shows what happens when you check.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
The Bottom Line
Deep research tools can make genuine contributions to historical research. They can also fabricate an entire scholarly consensus out of distorted numbers and garbled citations. Both outputs look equally authoritative. The difference is not visible in the format, the tone, or the footnotes. It is only visible when you do what historians are trained to do: go to the source and check.
The question is always the same: Interesting, I wonder if that is true.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
Footnotes


Cameron Blevins, “Generative AI and History in 2025,” December 19, 2025, https://cblevins.github.io/posts/gen-ai-history-2025/.↩︎

title: Getting Started
text:
You can begin with either of these free options. Both require only an email address to create an account:

ChatGPT (chatgpt.com) — Made by OpenAI. The free tier gives you access to the GPT-4o model with some usage limits. This is probably the most widely used LLM.
Google Gemini (gemini.google.com) — Made by Google. Free with a Google account. Comparable capability to ChatGPT for most tasks.

Both tools work in a web browser. You type a message, the model responds, and you can continue the conversation. There is nothing to install.
For this guide, the examples use ChatGPT, but the prompts work in any tool with minor adjustments.

title: Getting Started
text:
Your First Conversation
Open one of the tools above and try the following prompt. Copy and paste it, or adapt it to a topic you know well:

I am a historian studying the development of the western Canadian grain trade in the early twentieth century. Can you give me a brief overview of the major developments between 1900 and 1930, focusing on the role of grain elevators and the Winnipeg Grain Exchange?

Read the response. Some of it will be accurate. Some of it may be vague or slightly wrong. Notice:

Does it cite specific sources? (Probably not, or if it does, check whether they exist.)
Does it capture the broad outlines correctly?
Where does it feel generic — like a textbook summary rather than a historian’s analysis?

This is a useful calibration exercise. You now have a sense of what the tool knows and does not know about a topic you understand.

title: Getting Started
text:
A More Useful Exercise: Transcription
LLMs are often more useful for concrete tasks than for open-ended questions. Try this:

Find a page from a digitized historical document — a handwritten letter, a printed government report with difficult typography, or a census page. If you have a PDF on your computer, many LLM tools now allow you to upload images or documents directly.
Upload or paste the text and ask:


Please transcribe the text in this image as accurately as possible. This is a [type of document] from [approximate date and context]. Preserve the original spelling and punctuation.


Compare the output against the original. Where does it succeed? Where does it struggle?

For printed text in good condition, LLMs are often very accurate. For handwriting, the best models (particularly Gemini 3 via the API) now achieve accuracy comparable to expert human typists on English-language documents.1 Results vary by model, language, and document condition, but the gap between LLM transcription and human transcription has narrowed dramatically.

title: Getting Started
text:
Understanding the Conversation Model
A few things are worth knowing about how these tools work in practice:
Context window. The model can only “remember” a certain amount of text within a single conversation. If your conversation becomes very long, the model may lose track of things you said earlier. For long tasks, it can help to start a new conversation and restate the key context.
System prompts and instructions. You can begin a conversation by telling the model what role you want it to play or what constraints to follow. For example:

You are helping me transcribe 19th-century French colonial correspondence. The documents are from the Archives nationales d’outre-mer. Please preserve the original French text and flag any words you are uncertain about with [?].

This kind of framing improves results significantly.
Follow-up questions. You can refine the model’s output by asking follow-up questions in the same conversation:

That transcription looks mostly right, but I think the word you read as “commerce” in line 3 is actually “commode.” Can you look at that section again?

The model will adjust. This iterative back-and-forth is often more productive than trying to get a perfect result on the first try.

title: Getting Started
text:
What You Have Learned
After fifteen minutes with a free tool, you should have a sense of:

How the conversational interface works
What kind of output the model produces for topics you know
How transcription assistance works in practice
The importance of checking everything

The next section, Research Tasks, walks through specific applications in more detail.

title: Deep Research Example
text:
The following report was generated by Gemini Deep Research (Google) in March 2026, using this prompt:

I am a historian beginning a research project on the role of marine insurance in the British Atlantic slave trade, roughly 1700–1807. Please search for the key secondary literature and major scholarly debates. I am particularly interested in work published since 2000, particularly at the intersection of financial history and the history of slavery. Provide full bibliographic details for each source and a brief summary of its argument.

The output is reproduced here exactly as Gemini produced it, with formatting cleaned up but no content altered. It is included as a teaching resource — an example of what deep research tools can do, and a demonstration of why every claim and citation must be independently verified before use in scholarly work.






This Report Has Not Been Fact-Checked



The citations and claims below have not been verified against library catalogues, JSTOR, or the original sources. Some citations may be accurate; others may be fabricated or inaccurately described. This is the point of the example: the report looks authoritative, reads fluently, and may contain errors that are invisible without independent verification. See our Deep Research Tools page for a verification workflow.

title: Deep Research Example
section: Financial Underpinnings of the Middle Passage: Marine Insurance and the British Atlantic Slave Trade (1700–1807)
text:
Financial Underpinnings of the Middle Passage: Marine Insurance and the British Atlantic Slave Trade (1700–1807)

Introduction: Capitalism, Risk, and the Business of Slavery
The historical intersection of financial capitalism and the transatlantic slave trade has undergone a profound historiographical reappraisal over the course of the last two decades. While the foundational thesis linking the immense profits of colonial slavery to the broader economic development of the British Empire was established by Eric Williams in 1944, recent scholarship has increasingly turned its analytical focus toward the specific, highly specialized financial instruments that lubricated this brutal commerce. Among these instruments, marine insurance emerges as a critical, yet historically underexplored, technology of risk mitigation. Between 1700 and the abolition of the British slave trade in 1807, the forced transportation of an estimated 3.2 million enslaved Africans across the Atlantic by British vessels was fraught with immense financial peril for European investors and merchants. The highly volatile nature of the trade — characterized by fluctuating coastal markets, the constant threat of shipboard insurrection, catastrophic mortality rates among both captives and crew, and the unpredictable hazards of transoceanic navigation — required sophisticated institutional mechanisms to transform sheer Knightian uncertainty into calculable, manageable risk.
Marine insurance provided the indispensable means by which slaving merchants could distribute the localized, catastrophic risks of individual slave-trading voyages across a much broader network of domestic and international underwriters. In doing so, marine underwriting became an essential, load-bearing pillar of the Atlantic plantation complex. Without the structural guarantee of financial indemnity against the loss of ships and human “cargo,” the massive, dynamic expansion of the British slave trade in the eighteenth century would have been structurally and economically untenable. The capability of ship owners to discover methods to navigate the unique risks peculiar to the trade — which encompassed high morbidity rates, exchange value uncertainties, the moral hazard of crew negligence, and the profound threat of violent African resistance — was entirely dependent on the existence of a robust insurance market.
This report provides an exhaustive, granular analysis of the key secondary literature published since 2000 concerning the role of marine insurance in the British Atlantic slave trade. By synthesizing the major scholarly debates, this document explores the macroeconomic importance of slave insurance to the British financial sector, the institutional architectures of corporate and private underwriting, the evolution of contractual risk models, and the profound legal, moral, and epistemological paradoxes generated by the insurance of human beings as commercial property.


The Macroeconomic Debate: The Scale and Importance of Slave Trade Insurance
A central, highly contested debate within the contemporary historiography of the British slave trade revolves around the macroeconomic footprint of slavery-related marine insurance. Scholars continue to debate the precise extent to which the financial demands of the transatlantic slave trade acted as the primary catalyst for the development of the modern British marine insurance market, versus the perspective that the slave trade simply represented one lucrative sector within a much broader, diversified commercial expansion.

The Inikori Hypothesis and the Dominance Thesis
The modern macroeconomic debate was largely initiated by Joseph E. Inikori’s seminal and provocative work, Africans and the Industrial Revolution in England (2002). Inikori advanced the argument that the explosive growth of the British marine insurance sector was largely dependent upon, and fundamentally driven by, the business of underwriting slaving voyages and the closely related West Indian plantation trades. Relying on broad macroeconomic projections, Inikori estimated that the slave trade and the broader “Atlantic slave economy” comprised the vast majority of the premium income for British insurers during the critical decades of the late eighteenth century. To arrive at these striking figures — sometimes cited as accounting for up to 63 percent of premiums earned by the British marine insurance industry — Inikori utilized a back-projection methodology. This method was anchored in the testimony of John Julius Angerstein, a prominent Lloyd’s underwriter, who provided evidence to the Select Committee on Marine Insurance in 1810, stating that total British marine insurance premiums amounted to £10.95 million in 1809. By extrapolating backwards from this 1809 figure, Inikori constructed a model suggesting that slavery-related underwriting was the undisputed engine of the City of London’s insurance market.


Methodological Critiques and Archival Corrections
However, subsequent generations of financial historians have vigorously challenged Inikori’s methodology and his sweeping conclusions. Robin Pearson and David Richardson, in their definitive 2019 study “Insuring the Transatlantic Slave Trade,” highlight severe methodological flaws in Inikori’s back-projection techniques. While Pearson and Richardson agree that insurance was an absolutely vital risk mitigation device that facilitated the growth of the slave trade, their rigorous analysis of surviving merchant and brokerage archives demonstrates that direct slave voyages constituted a much smaller percentage of total U.K. marine insurance premiums than Inikori originally claimed.
Nicholas Draper has similarly contested the Inikori dominance thesis through meticulous corporate archival research. Through an analysis of the London Assurance Corporation (LAC) records from the period of 1769 to 1770, Draper demonstrates that approximately 15 percent of all sums insured on marine policies, and exactly one-third of total premiums, derived from the African and West Indian trades. While these are highly significant figures that underscore the deep complicity of the financial sector, they remain well below the overwhelming majorities estimated by Inikori for the 1790s.


Micro-Level Analysis of Merchant Outlays
To understand the true economic weight of marine insurance, Pearson and Richardson shifted the focus from macroeconomic modeling to micro-level, voyage-by-voyage empirical analysis. By examining the largest dataset ever compiled for slave trade insurance, they revealed the precise financial burden that risk mitigation placed on individual slaving operations.
Their research analyzed the accounts of William Davenport, a prominent Liverpool slave trader, who operated 74 slaving voyages between 1757 and 1784. The surviving records indicate that every single one of Davenport’s voyages was fully insured. Similarly, all 55 slaving voyages undertaken by James Rogers of Bristol between 1784 and 1793 carried comprehensive marine insurance. From this data, historians conclude that mid-eighteenth-century slave traders regularly and systematically insured their ventures; it was not a peripheral choice, but a core operational requirement.
Furthermore, Pearson and Richardson calculated the precise proportion of outlay costs accounted for by insurance premiums. For Davenport’s 74 voyages out of Liverpool, insurance premiums constituted 8.6 percent of total outlay costs. Across a broader sample of 60 “African ventures” from 1757 to 1806, insurance premiums accounted for 9.8 percent of outward voyage costs. Consequently, during the second half of the eighteenth century, insurance premiums routinely amounted to nearly 10 percent of the total capital required to launch a slaving voyage, a ratio that spiked even higher during periods of international wartime conflict when the threat of capture by privateers surged.


Re-evaluating Market Share: Direct Voyages vs. Bilateral Trade
The current scholarly consensus, forged through the meticulous archival recovery of individual underwriting books and corporate ledgers, presents a more nuanced, dualistic picture of the marine insurance market. Rather than a singular dominance of direct slave voyages (the outbound leg to Africa and the infamous Middle Passage to the Americas), the true, massive financial windfall for London’s insurers came from the broader, bilateral plantation economy.
Recent estimations compiled by institutional historians and scholars indicate that the insurance of direct slave voyages accounted for roughly 5 to 10 percent of total marine insurance premiums in the latter half of the eighteenth century. However, the vessels returning from the Caribbean and the Americas were of far greater financial importance to the City of London. These custom-built “West Indiamen” ships — capable of carrying far more bulk cargo than the specialized slave-ships — transported vital plantation supplies to the colonies and returned heavily laden with slave-grown produce such as sugar, tobacco, rum, cotton, coffee, indigo, and mahogany. The insurance on these returning voyages and the direct bilateral colonial trades accounted for approximately 30 percent of total marine insurance premiums paid.
When combined, the overarching slavery-related business — comprising both the direct trafficking of human beings and the transportation of the commodities they were forced to produce — is reliably estimated to have accounted for between 33 percent and 40 percent of all British premium income in the second half of the eighteenth century.


Table 1: Comparative Historiographical Estimates









Scholar / Source
Analytical Focus
Estimated Market Share
Methodological Approach




Inikori (2002)
Total Atlantic Slave Economy
Up to 63% of total marine premiums (widely disputed)

Draper (2008, 2010)
London Assurance Corporation

Pearson & Richardson (2019)
Individual Slave Trader Outlays
Premiums = ~8.6–10% of total voyage outlay costs
Micro-level analysis of merchant accounts (Davenport, Rogers)


Lloyd’s Historical Research
Overall Market Breakdown
5–10% from direct slave voyages; ~30% from bilateral plantation trade
Synthesis of institutional research on the broader slave economy






Institutional Architectures: Corporations, Coffee Houses, and Private Underwriters
The facilitation of the transatlantic slave trade required highly organized financial hubs capable of absorbing massive, concentrated liabilities. During the eighteenth century, the British marine insurance market was characterized by a distinct structural division: a heavily regulated corporate duopoly operating alongside a vast, decentralized, and dynamic network of private underwriters.

The Corporate Duopoly: London Assurance and Royal Exchange
Following the financial catastrophe of the South Sea Bubble, the British Parliament passed the Bubble Act of 1720, which strictly restricted corporate marine underwriting to two newly chartered companies: the London Assurance Corporation (LAC) and the Royal Exchange Assurance (REA). These institutions provided foundational, legally sanctioned underwriting capacity for early commercial ventures into the Atlantic world.
Surviving records from the Royal African Company (RAC) — the nation’s premier, albeit declining, slave-trading monopoly in the early eighteenth century — reveal an extensive reliance on these two corporations. An ‘Account of Insurances Made’ by the RAC in the early 1720s demonstrates that the monopoly held thirty-one active policies with the LAC and REA, insuring slave ships and trade goods for a substantial £116,000.
Despite their prestigious chartered status, the corporate duopoly actually accounted for a surprisingly small proportion of the total British marine insurance market. By the early nineteenth century, the combined market share of the LAC and REA fluctuated between merely 2 percent and 18.4 percent. The true, massive engine of slave trade underwriting lay outside the corporate boardrooms, in the coffee houses of the City of London.


The Centrality of Lloyd’s of London and Private Underwriting
The vast majority of marine insurance in the eighteenth century was written by private individuals operating within syndicates, for whom Lloyd’s of London functioned as the critical organizing platform. Operating initially out of Edward Lloyd’s original coffee house on Lombard Street (established circa 1688) and later moving to the Royal Exchange as the formally organized “New Lloyd’s” in 1771, this decentralized market rapidly became the undisputed global centre for insuring the shipping industry that facilitated the Middle Passage.
Recent institutional historiography, particularly the groundbreaking Underwriting Souls project spearheaded by Alexandre White at Johns Hopkins University (2023–2025), has forensically mapped the dense networks of complicity at Lloyd’s. White’s detailed analysis of the Lloyd’s archives demonstrates that the market was not merely passively involved, but deeply integrated into the operational structures of the transatlantic slave trade.
An analysis of the 77 surviving names from the critical 1771 list of New Lloyd’s founders reveals the profound overlap between the financial sector and the plantation economy. At least eight of these founding subscribers were direct, active investors in slave-trading voyages. A further seven subscribers were themselves slave-owners or held vast financial assets, such as colonial loans and annuities, that were legally secured by the bodies of enslaved people.
The involvement of Lloyd’s did not cleanly cease with the 1807 abolition of the British slave trade or the subsequent 1833 abolition of colonial slavery. The market seamlessly transitioned to underwriting the global trade in slave-grown commodities, providing insurance for cotton grown by enslaved people in the antebellum United States until 1865, and for sugar produced by enslaved labour in the Empire of Brazil until 1888.



The Epistemologies of Risk and Uncertainty: Operational Dynamics
To underwrite the transatlantic slave trade was to engage in the constant, ruthless recalibration of risk. European slave traders and their metropolitan insurers had to navigate a labyrinth of financial and operational perils.

Mitigating Voyage Volatility: The Morice Strategies
Matthew David Mitchell’s exhaustive monograph The Prince of Slavers (2020) provides crucial insights into how operational risk was managed during the transition from monopoly trading to private enterprise in the early 1700s. Analyzing the extensive career of Humphry Morice — a Member of Parliament, Governor of the Bank of England, and the largest-volume British slave trader of his era who forcibly transported over 30,000 enslaved Africans across 103 voyages — Mitchell demonstrates how private merchants developed innovative “best practices” to mitigate the inherent risks of the trade.
Morice frequently employed a highly complex multiple-ship strategy. By utilizing smaller craft, such as yawls, to navigate shallow African river systems and barter for captives rapidly, he reduced the time his primary vessel spent exposed to disease, depreciation, and rebellion on the coast.


Insuring Against Anticolonial Resistance
The pervasive threat of shipboard rebellion — which historical data indicates occurred on an estimated one in ten, to perhaps one in five, slaving voyages — forced insurers to constantly alter the scope of their financial coverage. Because enslaved people, unlike passive commodities such as sugar or tobacco, possessed human agency and continuously fought against their captors, underwriters had to price the risk of African resistance.
Scholars such as Alexandre White argue that the marine insurance archive, despite its colonial and financial ontology, paradoxically serves as a vital repository documenting anticolonial action. Insurers demanded higher premiums to cover the losses incurred during an insurrection, and pricing guidance directly influenced how slave ships were physically designed, heavily armed, and crewed to deal with the inevitable possibility of revolt.



The Legal Fictions of Human Cargo: The Zong and Jurisprudential Crisis
The legal architecture of marine insurance inherently required the categorization of living, breathing enslaved humans as inanimate commercial goods. This profound legal fiction produced horrific, violent outcomes when the abstract logic of the insurance contract collided with the material realities of the Middle Passage.

The Zong Massacre and Gregson v. Gilbert (1783)
In August 1781, the British slave ship Zong, owned by the Gregson syndicate of Liverpool, departed West Africa severely overcrowded with 442 enslaved people — more than twice its safe capacity. Delayed by gross navigational errors and purportedly facing a severe water shortage, the ship’s captain, Luke Collingwood, made a calculated, financially driven decision. Over several days, Collingwood ordered his crew to systematically throw 133 living, sick enslaved people overboard to their deaths.
This atrocity was, fundamentally, an act of insurance fraud. Under the standard terms of eighteenth-century marine insurance, if enslaved individuals died of disease, dehydration, or starvation in the ship’s hold, it was legally classified as a “natural death,” and the entire financial loss was borne exclusively by the ship’s owners. However, under the deeply entrenched maritime legal principle of “general average,” if a portion of the “cargo” had to be intentionally jettisoned to save the ship and the remaining crew from an existential threat, the loss could be legally claimed against the underwriters as a legitimate “peril of the sea.” Collingwood knew there was a poor market for sick captives in Jamaica; by murdering them, he sought to realize their full insured value.
The resulting trial, Gregson v. Gilbert (1783), was heard before Lord Chief Justice Mansfield at London’s Guildhall. While a jury initially ruled in favour of the shipowners, Mansfield ordered a retrial upon discovering new evidence suggesting that the severe water shortage was not an act of God, but a direct result of the captain’s gross mismanagement and navigational incompetence. Ultimately, the owners did not receive the insurance payout. However, the legal proceedings treated the mass murder of 133 people entirely as a routine civil dispute over property, contract terms, and maritime law, utterly ignoring the humanity of the victims and the criminality of the act.


The Redefinition of Liability: Tatham v. Hodgson (1796)
The public outrage generated by the Zong and similar maritime abuses forced a rapid legal and statutory redefinition of insurance liabilities. In Tatham v. Hodgson (1796), the court determined that the starvation or death of slaves due to insufficient provisions — even if those catastrophic shortages were caused by extraordinary delays resulting from bad weather — constituted “natural death” and was strictly not recoverable as a “peril of the sea.” This critical judicial ruling was subsequently codified into British statutory law, and the insurance of enslaved people on British vessels was outlawed entirely in 1807, concurrent with the passage of the act that abolished the British slave trade.



Annotated Analysis of Key Secondary Literature (2000–2025)

Inikori, Joseph E. Africans and the Industrial Revolution in England: A Study in International Trade and Economic Development. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2002.

Inikori’s massive macroeconomic study is the foundational text that sparked the modern debate regarding the scale of slave trade insurance. Using back-projection methods derived from 1809 Lloyd’s data, he controversially estimated that the slaving economy accounted for upwards of 63 percent of British premium income. While his specific projections have been heavily revised downward, his central thesis — that slavery was the indispensable engine of British financial modernization — remains a cornerstone of the field.

Baucom, Ian. Specters of the Atlantic: Finance Capital, Slavery, and the Philosophy of History. Durham: Duke University Press, 2005.

Operating at the intersection of critical theory, literary analysis, and financial history, Baucom analyzes the Zong massacre to chart the epistemological evolution of global capitalism. He argues that the marine insurance industry drove a speculative, abstract shift in finance capital that subjugated material human life to the concept of monetary equivalence.

Rupprecht, Anita. “‘A Very Uncommon Case’: Representations of the Zong and the British Campaign to Abolish the Slave Trade.” Journal of Legal History 28, no. 3 (2007): 329–346.

Rupprecht challenges existing treatments of the Zong massacre by returning to the unpublished trial proceedings at the National Maritime Museum. She argues that the abolitionist movement actively stripped the event of its complex insurance logic to craft a sentimentalized narrative of suffering.

Draper, Nicholas. The Price of Emancipation: Slave-Ownership, Compensation and British Society at the End of Slavery. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2010.

Draper utilizes London Assurance Corporation data to refute the extreme upper bounds of Inikori’s dominance thesis, while confirming that roughly one-third of corporate insurance premiums derived from the African and West Indian trades.

Pearson, Robin, and David Richardson. “Insuring the Transatlantic Slave Trade.” The Journal of Economic History 79, no. 2 (June 2019): 417–446.

The most rigorous quantitative analysis of slave trade insurance to date. Utilizing the largest dataset ever compiled, Pearson and Richardson prove that insurance was ubiquitous (~10% of voyage outlays) and decisively refute Inikori’s macroeconomic estimates for direct voyages.

Mitchell, Matthew David. The Prince of Slavers: Humphry Morice and the Transformation of Britain’s Transatlantic Slave Trade, 1698–1732. Palgrave Macmillan, 2020.

A micro-historical analysis of Humphry Morice, the largest-volume British slave trader of the early eighteenth century. Mitchell details how private merchants innovated operational risk management strategies that directly shaped the contractual clauses adopted by the marine insurance market.

Hope, Mallory. “Underwriting the Slave Trade: Risk, Uncertainty, and Marine Insurance in the Eighteenth-Century French Atlantic.” International Journal of Maritime History, 2023.

Hope advances the field through comparative analysis of standard legal merchant manuals against actual surviving manuscript insurance contracts, demonstrating how merchants and underwriters engaged in strategic language games to manipulate financial exposure.

White, Alexandre, et al. Underwriting Souls (Digital Exhibition and Scholarship). Johns Hopkins University / Mellon Foundation, 2023–2024.

A groundbreaking digital humanities project based on unprecedented access to the Lloyd’s of London archives, documenting how underwriters mathematically modelled the risks of African resistance and insurrection.



Table 2: Quick Reference








Author(s) & Year
Title
Core Methodological Contribution




Inikori (2002)
Africans and the Industrial Revolution
Macroeconomic back-projection establishing the dominance thesis


Baucom (2005)
Specters of the Atlantic
Critical theory analysis of the Zong and the epistemology of finance


Rupprecht (2007)
“‘A Very Uncommon Case’”
Legal history decoupling the Zong trial from abolitionist mythology


Draper (2010)
The Price of Emancipation
Corporate archival sampling (LAC) correcting premium estimates


Pearson & Richardson (2019)
“Insuring the Transatlantic Slave Trade”
Micro-level merchant outlays proving ubiquity of insurance


Mitchell (2020)
The Prince of Slavers
Biography of Morice mapping early operational risk innovations


Hope (2023)
“Underwriting the Slave Trade”
Contractual analysis of Knightian risk vs. uncertainty


White (2023–2024)
Underwriting Souls
Digital humanities excavation of the Lloyd’s archive






Conclusion: The Enduring Legacy of Underwriting Souls
The exhaustive historical investigation into marine insurance and the British Atlantic slave trade reveals a profound, inseparable symbiosis between the development of modern financial capitalism and the systematic commodification of human life. The robust secondary literature published since 2000 conclusively demonstrates that while direct Middle Passage voyages may not have monopolized the entire ledger of British marine insurance as Joseph Inikori originally hypothesized, the broader Atlantic slave economy was an indispensable, load-bearing engine for the growth of metropolitan financial institutions. For the individual European slave trader, securing a marine insurance policy was not merely a peripheral financial precaution; it was an absolute structural necessity that insulated merchants against the catastrophic, existential volatility of oceanic transit, epidemic disease, and the pervasive, terrifying threat of African resistance.
Today, the historiography of slave insurance has matured significantly, moving far beyond the simple macroeconomic quantification of premiums to encompass the cultural, theoretical, and digital excavation of the archive itself. The meticulous historical reconstruction of these eighteenth-century underwriting practices serves as a vital, permanent reminder that the advanced technologies of risk mitigation — the very bedrock of contemporary global finance — were forged, in significant part, through the systematic pricing of human misery and the relentless, actuarial calculation of captive survival.

title: Copy Editing
text:
There is a difference between asking an LLM to write for you and asking it to edit what you have already written. This guide is about the latter. The assumption is that you have drafted your own prose — the arguments, the structure, the evidence are yours — and you want help catching errors, tightening sentences, and improving clarity.
This is not meaningfully different from asking a colleague to read a draft, or from using Grammarly, or from working with a copy editor. The tool is more capable than a spell-checker and less capable than a good human editor. It is useful precisely in that middle range.

title: Copy Editing
text:
There is a difference between asking an LLM to write for you and asking it to edit what you have already written. This guide is about the latter. The assumption is that you have drafted your own prose — the arguments, the structure, the evidence are yours — and you want help catching errors, tightening sentences, and improving clarity.
This is not meaningfully different from asking a colleague to read a draft, or from using Grammarly, or from working with a copy editor. The tool is more capable than a spell-checker and less capable than a good human editor. It is useful precisely in that middle range.

title: Copy Editing
text:
How to Do It

Basic Proofreading
Paste a section of your draft and ask:

Please proofread the following passage for spelling, grammar, and punctuation errors. Do not change the content, argument, or voice — only flag mechanical errors. List each error with the original text and the suggested correction.

This produces a list of corrections you can accept or reject individually, rather than a rewritten passage that you have to compare line by line.


Clarity and Flow

Please read the following passage and suggest improvements to clarity and readability. Do not change the argument or the evidence. Focus on sentence structure, word choice, and flow. Present your suggestions as a list of specific changes, not a rewritten version.



Consistency Checking

I am writing a chapter that uses the following terms: “enslaved person” (not “slave”), “slaveholder” (not “slave owner”), and “plantation” (not “estate” except when referring to legal documents). Please check the following passage for consistency with these preferences and flag any deviations.



Citation and Footnote Review

Please check the following passage for any footnotes that appear to reference the wrong source, or for claims that are made without a supporting footnote. I have pasted the passage with its footnotes below.

title: Copy Editing
text:
Staying in Control
The key risk with AI-assisted editing is that the tool may “improve” your prose in ways that change your meaning or flatten your voice. A few guidelines:

Always review changes individually. Do not accept a batch rewrite. Ask for a list of specific suggestions, not a new version of the paragraph.
Reject stylistic changes that do not suit you. LLMs tend toward a generic, slightly formal tone. If the model suggests replacing your phrasing with something blander, keep yours.
Watch for meaning drift. The model may tighten a sentence in a way that subtly changes what you are saying. Read each suggestion against your intended meaning.
Do not let it restructure your argument. If you ask for “clarity” suggestions and the model proposes reordering your paragraphs, that is an intellectual decision, not a copy-editing one. Make those choices yourself.

title: Copy Editing
text:
What It Does Well

Catching typos and grammatical errors, including subtle ones (subject-verb agreement across long sentences, misplaced modifiers)
Identifying repetitive word choice
Flagging inconsistent formatting (dates, capitalization, hyphenation)
Spotting missing articles or prepositions that a spell-checker would miss

title: Copy Editing
text:
What It Does Poorly

Recognizing intentional stylistic choices (sentence fragments for emphasis, deliberate repetition, rhetorical structure)
Understanding disciplinary conventions (Chicago-style footnotes, the use of the historical present, block quotation formatting)
Knowing when not to change something

title: Copy Editing
text:
A Practical Workflow

Write your draft yourself. Do not use GenAI for initial drafting.
Revise your draft yourself. Get the argument and structure where you want them.
Run a proofreading pass with the LLM, asking for a list of mechanical errors.
Accept or reject each suggestion individually.
Optionally, run a clarity pass — again reviewing each suggestion against your original intent.
Read the final version yourself. You are responsible for every word.

This workflow keeps you in the author’s seat throughout. The LLM functions as a very fast, very patient proofreader — nothing more.

title: Deep Research Example: Algonquin Land Claim

Unlike the marine insurance example, where verification revealed systematic fabrication, this report surfaced genuinely important sources that the researcher had not encountered in three years of conventional archival work.
