This Report Contains Both Genuine Discoveries and Potential Errors



The key sources identified in this report — particularly Brian Lawson’s 2009 conference paper and the Government of Canada publication — were verified and proved to be real and relevant. title: Limitations and Risks
text: The preceding pages describe what GenAI tools can do. This section describes what they cannot do, what they do badly, and where using them can lead you astray. For a historian, understanding the failure modes is not optional — it is the difference between a useful tool and a liability.

title: Limitations and Risks
text: The preceding pages describe what GenAI tools can do. This section describes what they cannot do, what they do badly, and where using them can lead you astray. For a historian, understanding the failure modes is not optional — it is the difference between a useful tool and a liability.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Hallucination
This is the most important limitation. LLMs generate text that is statistically plausible, not text that is true. They will:

Invent citations. They produce author names, titles, journal names, volume numbers, and page ranges that look entirely real but correspond to no actual publication.
Fabricate historical details. They may generate plausible-sounding dates, names, or events that did not occur, especially for obscure topics.
Blend real and false information seamlessly. A paragraph might contain three accurate claims and one fabricated one, with no difference in tone or confidence.

The frequency of hallucinations has decreased significantly in the latest generation of models (2025–2026). This is genuine progress — but it creates a new danger. When earlier models hallucinated frequently, historians learned to check everything. When most output is accurate, complacency is the risk: you verify the first few citations, they all check out, and you stop looking. The one fabrication slips through. Reduced hallucination rates make verification more important, not less, because the cost of the errors you miss is higher when your guard is down.

What to Do About It

Verify every factual claim against a source you trust.
Verify every citation in a library catalogue, JSTOR, or Google Scholar.
Never cite an LLM as a source in scholarly work. It is not a source; it is a text-generation engine.
Be especially cautious about topics that are obscure, recent, or poorly represented in English-language digital text.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Bias and Representation
LLMs reflect the biases of their training data. For historians, this has specific implications:

Anglophone and Western bias. The models know more about the history of Britain and the United States than about the history of, say, the Swahili coast or Central Asia. Responses about less-represented topics will be thinner, more generic, and more likely to contain errors. This weakness has improved significantly since 2024, but they can not overcome the limits in published scholarship and digitization of primary sources.
Historiographic lag. The training data reflects the published literature, which means the models tend to reproduce older historiographic framings. They may default to nationalist narratives, Great Man history, or other interpretive frameworks that the discipline has moved beyond. Some of the companies appear to have more access to paywalled and copyrighted material than others.
Erasure and euphemism. Some models are tuned to avoid controversial topics, which can result in euphemistic or evasive language about slavery, colonialism, genocide, and other subjects that historians must discuss directly. Prompts matter here and a prompt using language related to settler colonialism or genocide scholarship will generally create better responces than more generic prompts that might default to Wikipedia level answers. n


What to Do About It

Treat LLM output as you would an encyclopedia entry: a starting point that reflects a particular perspective, not a neutral authority.
Be alert to gaps — what the model does not say can be as revealing as what it does.
If you notice the model using euphemistic language about sensitive historical topics, push back explicitly in your prompt: “Please use direct, scholarly language. Do not euphemize.”

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
The Illusion of Understanding
LLMs produce text that reads as if it were written by someone who understands the topic. Whether the model truly “understands” is a philosophical question that remains unresolved, but the practical consequence matters: current models can, in many cases, distinguish stronger arguments from weaker ones, identify relevant evidence, and construct plausible interpretations. They have become surprisingly good at synthesis and historiographic analysis.
The danger has shifted. The risk is no longer that the model produces obviously shallow output — it often does not. The risk is that the output is good enough to be persuasive but subtly wrong in ways that require deep domain knowledge to catch: a mischaracterized argument, evidence from the wrong context, a framing that flattens genuine historiographic debate. The tasks that define historical practice — weighing evidence, assessing significance, judging competing interpretations — can be assisted by LLMs but cannot be delegated to them. The historian remains responsible for the argument.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Privacy and Intellectual Property

Your Data
When you paste text into a web-based chatbot, that text is transmitted to the company’s servers. Consider:

Unpublished research. If you paste a chapter draft into ChatGPT, that text has left your control. Most services state that they do not use paid-tier conversations for training, but policies vary and change.
Sensitive data. Records involving living people, embargoed materials, or data covered by research ethics agreements should not be pasted into commercial LLM tools without careful consideration.
Student work. If you are using GenAI tools to evaluate student submissions, be aware that you may be sharing student work with a third-party service.



The Model’s “Knowledge”
LLMs were trained on text scraped from the internet, including copyrighted material. The legal and ethical status of this training is unresolved and actively litigated. This is worth being aware of, though it does not directly affect most research use cases.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Over-Reliance
The subtlest risk is not that the tool will fail dramatically but that it will work well enough to change your behaviour. Historians who use LLMs for translation may gradually do less of their own translation practice. Historians who use LLMs for literature discovery may search databases less thoroughly. Historians who use LLMs for coding may understand their own scripts less deeply.
None of these are inevitable outcomes, but they are real risks. The best safeguard is intentionality: decide in advance what you want to learn and what you want to delegate, and revisit that decision periodically.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Summary

Summary of limitations and mitigations






Risk
Mitigation




Hallucinated citations
Verify every reference — especially when most check out


Fabricated facts
Check all claims against trusted sources


Bias and gaps
Treat output as a starting point, not an authority


Privacy concerns
Do not paste sensitive or unpublished material into free-tier tools


Over-reliance
Decide consciously what to delegate; maintain your own skills


Persuasive but subtly wrong analysis
The historian evaluates and takes responsibility for all arguments

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
The Bottom Line
These tools are useful — and considerably more capable than they were even a year ago. They can now assist with genuinely intellectual tasks: synthesis, interpretation, historiographic analysis, argument construction. But “assist” is the operative word. The historian evaluates the output, takes responsibility for the argument, and decides what holds up. The tools have moved beyond mere mechanical assistance, but the judgement — the reading, the thinking, the weighing of evidence — remains yours.
That is not a limitation of the technology. It is the nature of the discipline.

title: Negotiating Sovereignty, Contesting Territory: The Algonquin, L’Isle-aux-Allumettes, and Upper Canada, 1835-1839
text:
Negotiating Sovereignty, Contesting Territory: The Algonquin, L’Isle-aux-Allumettes, and Upper Canada, 1835-1839
I. Introduction
A. Overview of the 1835-1836 Negotiation
In 1835, Algonquin and Nipissing leaders travelled to Toronto, the capital of Upper Canada, to negotiate with Lieutenant-Governor Sir John Colborne. Their objective was to secure recognition of their territorial rights in the Ottawa Valley and establish a reserve, focusing specifically on L’Isle-aux-Allumettes and adjacent lands along the Ottawa River. Having received what they understood to be a favourable response, they returned in 1836 to confirm the arrangement with the new Lieutenant-Governor, Sir Francis Bond Head. However, Head revoked the nascent agreement, citing a pre-existing treaty with the Mississauga Nation as justification for denying the Algonquin claim to the lands south of the island. This sequence of events—an apparent agreement followed by abrupt revocation based on a contested interpretation of another treaty—represents a critical and revealing episode in the complex history of Algonquin-Crown relations and the administration of Indigenous affairs in 19th-century Canada. It highlights the vulnerability of Indigenous land rights in the face of colonial expansion, shifting political administrations, and the powerful influence of resource extraction interests.
B. Central Research Questions
This report examines the historical context, unfolding events, and immediate aftermath of the 1835-1836 negotiations. It seeks to answer several key questions: What were the historical and legal bases upon which the Algonquin and Nipissing founded their claim to L’Isle-aux-Allumettes and the surrounding Ottawa Valley territory? What specific terms were discussed with Lieutenant-Governor Colborne in 1835, and what commitments, if any, did the Crown make? Why did Lieutenant-Governor Head revoke this understanding in 1836, and how valid was the government’s reliance on a prior Mississauga treaty (the Crawford Purchase) to justify this decision? What do these events reveal about the nature of colonial governance, the interpretation of treaties and Indigenous rights, and the formulation of Indigenous policy in Upper Canada during the turbulent 1830s?
C. Methodology and Source Distinction
The analysis presented here relies on a careful examination of available primary source materials and secondary scholarly works. Primary sources include petitions submitted by Algonquin and Nipissing leaders, such as the detailed Memorial of June 6, 1835 , and correspondence from colonial officials documenting government responses and internal deliberations, like the 1836 letter detailing Colborne’s position. These are supplemented by records pertaining to the administrations of Lieutenant-Governors Colborne and Head , Executive Council minutes , and documents related to the Mississauga treaties, particularly the Crawford Purchase. Secondary sources encompass scholarly analyses focusing on Algonquin history, the Ottawa Valley timber trade, colonial land policies, and specific government reports like those of Justice Macauley (1839), the Bagot Commission (1842-44), and the Pennefather Commission (1856-58). Throughout this report, a concerted effort is made to clearly distinguish between information drawn directly from primary documents and interpretations offered by historical scholarship, ensuring transparency in the reconstruction of these events. The precarious nature of negotiated understandings that lacked the formal status of ratified treaties becomes evident through this examination; such arrangements proved highly susceptible to changes in colonial personnel and policy priorities.
D. Scope and Structure
This report focuses specifically on the 1835-1836 negotiations concerning L’Isle-aux-Allumettes and adjacent lands within the jurisdiction of Upper Canada. It situates these events within their deeper historical context, tracing Algonquin presence and rights assertions from the pre-contact era through the early 19th century. The core sections detail the 1835 petition and apparent agreement with Colborne, the 1836 revocation by Head, and the ensuing controversy surrounding the interpretation of the Mississauga treaty. Subsequent government responses and reports up to the late 1830s and early 1840s are examined to understand the immediate policy consequences. The report concludes with a synthesis of findings and an analysis of the significance of this episode.
II. Historical Context: Algonquin Presence and Claims in the Ottawa Valley
A. Deep Historical Roots
The Algonquin people possess an ancient connection to the Ottawa River Valley, with archaeological evidence indicating their presence for at least 8,000 years prior to European arrival. Their traditional territory historically encompassed the entire length of the Ottawa River (known in Anishinabemowin as Kitcisìpi, the Great River) and its vast watershed, straddling the present-day border between Ontario and Quebec. Among the distinct Algonquin groups inhabiting this region, the Kichesipirini (“People of the Great River” or, as the French called them, Nation de l’Isle or Savages of the Island) were prominently associated with Kitcesìpìr Mìnìs, now known as Allumette Island (L’Isle-aux-Allumettes). Traditionally, most Algonquin communities practiced a semi-nomadic lifestyle, moving seasonally to hunt, fish, trap, and gather resources, utilizing birchbark canoes for summer travel and snowshoes and toboggans in winter. The Kichesipirini, however, maintained a more permanent settlement on Allumette Island, leveraging its strategic location on the river to control trade and levy tolls on passing canoe flotillas. Archaeological findings on nearby Morrison Island reveal a thousand-year-old culture involved in copper tool manufacturing and extensive trade networks reaching north to Lake Superior and south towards New York.
B. Post-Contact Era: Alliances, Conflict, and Displacement
The arrival of Europeans profoundly disrupted Algonquin life. Samuel de Champlain encountered the Kichesipirini chief Tessouat in 1603 and later visited their island stronghold in 1613. Alliances were formed, primarily with the French, centered around the lucrative fur trade. However, these alliances, coupled with competition over resources, drew the Algonquin into devastating conflicts with the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois Confederacy), who were allied first with the Dutch and later the English. Compounding the violence were waves of infectious diseases introduced by Europeans, which decimated Indigenous populations. Around 1650, intense warfare led to the near-extermination of the Kichesipirini on Allumette Island by the Iroquois, and the island was largely abandoned by permanent Indigenous settlement for a considerable period, though not necessarily devoid of Algonquin use. Despite these catastrophic events, Algonquin communities persisted in the upper reaches of the Ottawa Valley and its tributaries, adapting to the changed circumstances. Some Algonquin groups relocated, with many converts settling first at Trois-Rivières and then Sillery, weakening the main body defending the Ottawa Valley trade route.
C. Assertion of Rights under British Rule (Post-1760)
Following the British conquest of New France, the relationship between the Crown and Indigenous peoples was formally outlined in the Royal Proclamation of 1763. This seminal document recognized existing Indigenous rights to land and established that lands could only be alienated through formal treaties made between the Crown and the Indigenous nations occupying them. The Algonquin participated in subsequent treaty councils, such as those at Swegatchy and Kahnawake, and received copies of the Proclamation, understanding it and related agreements (like Governor Carleton’s 1766 proclamation urging settlers to treat Indigenous people as “Friends & Brothers”) as guarantees of their rights and possession of their lands. Wampum belts held by the Algonquin commemorate these agreements and commitments.
As Loyalist settlement pressures increased after the American Revolution (1783), the Algonquin became increasingly concerned about encroachment on their traditional territories. They began petitioning the government, asserting their rights and requesting protection. A notable instance occurred in July 1794, when Algonquin and Nipissing chiefs met with Colonel John Campbell, Deputy Superintendent of the Indian Department, in Quebec. They explicitly complained about Iroquois incursions and settler pressure forcing other tribes onto their hunting grounds, stating unequivocally that they “had not sold our lands above Long Sault” on the Ottawa River. They detailed mistreatment by specific settlers like “Captain Fortune,” who restricted their access to fishing, hunting, and even firewood. While willing to accommodate limited settlement along the river (requesting grants be no more than forty arpents deep), they urgently pleaded that lands extending into their tributary rivers not be granted, as this would destroy their subsistence. They appealed to their status as the original, peaceful possessors,f these lands?“.
This consistent assertion of rights faced an administrative complication with the Constitutional Act of 1791, which established Upper and Lower Canada, using the Ottawa River as the boundary. This act arbitrarily divided the Algonquin homeland between two separate colonial jurisdictions, hindering unified administration and recognition of their claims.
The historical record demonstrates a continuous Algonquin assertion of rights based on ancestral occupation and Crown commitments like the Royal Proclamation, despite significant historical disruptions and administrative fragmentation. However, colonial authorities often viewed these claims through a different lens, sometimes emphasizing historical events like the 17th-century Iroquois conflicts or the 1791 administrative boundary to minimize or dismiss Algonquin title, particularly when it conflicted with settlement or resource interests. This reveals a persistent divergence between Indigenous understandings of ongoing stewardship and relationship to the land, and a colonial framework focused on discrete land cessions and jurisdictional lines.
Furthermore, the economic basis of the colonial relationship shifted significantly in the early 19th century. While the earlier fur trade economy valued Algonquin knowledge and control of river networks , the rise of the timber industry transformed the Ottawa Valley’s forests into commodities and fueled demand for land settlement. This transition, accelerating in the 1830s, placed intense pressure on Algonquin hunting grounds and resources, leading directly to the conflicts and dispossession that characterized the period. The Algonquins’ own petitions lamented the destruction caused by settlers and loggers, and later government reports implicitly acknowledged the timber industry’s role in necessitating Algonquin displacement.
III. The 1835 Petition and Negotiation with Lt. Governor Colborne
A. The Petition of June 6, 1835
Against this backdrop of increasing pressure and diminishing resources, Algonquin and Nipissing leaders sought direct intervention from the Crown’s representative. On June 6, 1835, they presented a”humble Memorial” to Major General Sir John Colborne, then Lieutenant-Governor of Upper Canada, during a visit to Toronto. Colborne held this post from 1828 until early 1836.
The text of the 1835 petition provides a clear articulation of their grievances and claims. The Chiefs and Warriors, speaking for their nations, asserted their “immemorial” occupation and enjoyment of hunting grounds on both sides of the Ottawa River up to Lake Nipissing. They pointedly stated that extensive land grants had been made and settlements established by Colborne’s predecessors on these territories—specifically mentioning the south side of the Ottawa within Upper Canada—without any purchase having been made or compensation offered, actions they viewed as contrary to the King’s promises, implicitly referencing the Royal Proclamation. They emphasized their unique vulnerability, noting that unlike other tribes, they possessed no lands yielding revenue and were “solely dependent upon the Chase on our Hunting Grounds for support and Maintenance.” This reliance was critically threatened, they argued, by the “rapidly increasing Settlement” and the “indiscriminate and wanton Destruction by the Whites or new Settlers of the Beaver and other Animals,” which would soon “annihilate our Chase.” Consequently, they felt “irresistibly compelled” to seek “reasonable Indemnification” for the lands already lost on the south side of the river and requested that the Crown “ratify and confirm” their “Use, Occupation, and Possession” of the hunting grounds still remaining to them on that side. Finally, they asked Colborne to command, by proclamation or otherwise, that all “Intruders or Squatters” be removed from these remaining lands.
B. Proposed Transition to Agriculture
While the 1835 petition text focuses on hunting rights and compensation for lost grounds, the context surrounding the negotiations indicates an awareness among the Algonquin leadership of the need to adapt their economy. The petition’s own description of the destruction of the hunt underscores the unsustainability of relying solely on traditional practices in the face of rapid settlement and resource depletion. Scholarly analysis suggests that the Algonquin proposed L’Isle-aux-Allumettes partly because its location offered potential for establishing a more settled agricultural base, alongside continued access to remaining hunting territories further upriver. This aligns with broader colonial policies of the era, which increasingly promoted agriculture and settlement as the desired future for Indigenous peoples, often as a precursor to assimilation.
C. The Focus on L’Isle-aux-Allumettes
L’Isle-aux-Allumettes emerged as the specific focal point for the proposed reserve. Situated further up the Ottawa River, it was relatively remote from the main thrust of settlement advancing westward from the St. Lawrence at that time. For the Algonquin, it represented a part of their ancestral territory where they might consolidate their community and adapt their way of life. According to Lawson’s analysis, the Algonquin even contemplated allowing existing non-Aboriginal squatters on the island to remain, provided they paid rent to the Algonquin Nation, suggesting a pragmatic approach aimed at securing both land tenure and a potential source of revenue.
D. Colborne’s Response (Apparent Agreement)
The Algonquin delegation seemingly left Toronto in 1835 with the impression that they had secured Colborne’s agreement, or at least his strong support. This perception is supported by subsequent communications from Colborne’s administration. A letter dated February 26, 1836, from Colonel James Givins, Chief Superintendent of the Indian Department in Toronto, to Lieutenant-Colonel D.C. Napier, Secretary for Indian Affairs in Quebec, conveyed Colborne’s position regarding the Algonquin and Nipissing petition. With respect to their request for securing possession of their remaining hunting grounds on the south side of the Ottawa, the letter stated: “His Excellency desires me to express his readiness to assist as far as possible, The Petitioners in settling on such part of those grounds as they may select, and as far as it may be practicable to protect them against The Intrusion of strangers”.
While seemingly positive, the documented commitment from Colborne’s administration contained significant ambiguities. The language used – “assist as far as possible,” allowing settlement on lands “as they may select,” and protection “as far as it may be practicable” – stopped short of explicitly confirming Algonquin title to L’Isle-aux-Allumettes or guaranteeing the specific reserve boundaries and indemnification they sought. The focus on facilitating settlement aligned with prevailing colonial objectives but did not fully address the Algonquin’s fundamental claims based on the Royal Proclamation and immemorial rights. This lack of precise, legally binding language left the arrangement vulnerable to reinterpretation or outright dismissal by Colborne’s successor.
IV. The 1836 Revocation under Lt. Governor Head
A. Change in Leadership
The political landscape in Upper Canada shifted significantly in early 1836. Sir John Colborne departed, and Sir Francis Bond Head arrived in Toronto in January to assume the role of Lieutenant-Governor. Head, a former military officer and Poor Law commissioner with little colonial experience, arrived during a period of intense political conflict between Reformers and the ruling elite (often dubbed the “Family Compact”). Although initially welcomed by Reformers, Head quickly alienated them through his partisan actions, refusal to consult his Executive Council, and eventual dissolution of the Reform-dominated Assembly, leading to a bitter election campaign in 1836 which he won by appealing to Loyalist sentiments. His administration was marked by confrontation and ultimately contributed to the outbreak of the Rebellion of 1837. This political context suggests an administrator preoccupied with internal colonial power struggles and potentially less inclined to engage sympathetically with complex Indigenous land claims that might impede settlement or resource development favored by his political allies.
Table 1: Key Individuals and Roles (c. 1835-1839)

B. The Algonquin Delegation to Toronto (1836)
Trusting in the commitments made the previous year, an Algonquin delegation returned to Toronto in 1836. They had consulted with their nation and were prepared to discuss the transition to agriculture, believing they were moving forward with the plan to establish a reserve at L’Isle-aux-Allumettes. They arrived to find Head newly installed as Lieutenant-Governor.
C. Head’s Revocation and Rationale
The meeting with Lieutenant-Governor Head proved disastrous for the Algonquin hopes. Head refused to honour the understanding reached with Colborne. His justification, as reported by the Algonquin and analyzed by scholars, was that the lands in question—specifically the south bank of the Ottawa River adjacent to L’Isle-aux-Allumettes—had already been surrendered to the Crown by the Mississauga Nation through previous treaties. Consequently, the Crown could not grant these lands to the Algonquin. Head effectively dismissed the Algonquin petition’s core claims regarding their own ancestral rights and the lack of prior cession by them. Instead of confirming a reserve or discussing compensation, he offered only potential assistance with resettlement elsewhere, mirroring the general policy direction favouring the concentration of Indigenous peoples away from settlement frontiers. This stance represented a complete reversal of the position communicated by Colborne’s administration just months earlier.
The timing and rationale behind Head’s decision suggest that the invocation of the Mississauga treaty may have served as a convenient justification to nullify Colborne’s potentially burdensome commitment. Head was embroiled in domestic political conflicts and aligned himself with forces favouring settlement and resource exploitation. Acknowledging Algonquin title and establishing a reserve, particularly one that might include rent-paying squatters as envisioned by the Algonquin , could hinder these objectives. Furthermore, the timber trade, a major economic engine in the Ottawa Valley, relied on access to the river and surrounding forests. Using an interpretation of a pre-existing treaty with a different Indigenous nation allowed Head to sidestep the specific claims and historical context presented by the Algonquin in their 1835 petition , thereby avoiding the creation of perceived impediments to colonial expansion under the guise of upholding a prior agreement.
V. The Mississauga Treaty Controversy
A. The Government’s Position (Post-1836)
Following Head’s 1836 decision, the official government position solidified: the lands along the south bank of the Ottawa River opposite L’Isle-aux-Allumettes, and arguably the island itself (given its proximity and the claim’s focus on adjacent lands), were deemed to have been previously ceded by the Mississauga. By 1837, officials explicitly stated that the island was considered part of southeastern lands already surrendered by the Mississauga Anishinabeg.
B. Identifying the Relevant Treaty - The Crawford Purchase (1783)
The specific agreement underpinning the government’s claim was the Crawford Purchase, negotiated in October 1783. This was one of several early land agreements made by the British Crown in the immediate aftermath of the American Revolution. Its primary purpose was to secure lands along the St. Lawrence River and the northeastern shore of Lake Ontario to resettle Loyalists, including Haudenosaunee allies, who had supported Britain during the war and were displaced from the newly formed United States. Captain William Crawford negotiated the purchase with Mississauga representatives.
C. The Crux of the Dispute: Boundary Ambiguity
The central issue enabling the government’s 1836 stance was the profound ambiguity surrounding the northern boundary of the Crawford Purchase. Captain Crawford’s own report on the agreement described the acquired territory as extending from the Toniato River (near Gananoque) westward to a river in the Bay of Quinte, “extending from the lake back as far as a man can travel in a day,” and including all islands. This description is inherently vague. The phrase “as far as a man can travel in a day” is imprecise and open to wide interpretation. Crucially, the Ottawa River is not explicitly mentioned as the northern boundary in the primary accounts of the purchase.
This ambiguity persisted long after 1783. Later government documents and maps displayed conflicting interpretations of the purchase’s extent. Significantly, an 1847 map titled “Plan shewing the different surrenders made by the Indians in Upper Canada to the Crown” depicted the area presumed to be the Crawford Purchase as unceded land. Furthermore, historical accounts note that another Indigenous leader, Chief Mynass from Kanesatake (Oka), also claimed territory overlapping the Crawford Purchase area and extending north to the Ottawa River, adding another layer of complexity and contested ownership recognized by some contemporaneous officials. The irregularity of these early purchases, conducted under pressure to secure land quickly for refugees and potentially facilitated by inducements like rum, was significant.
D. The Algonquin Counter-Argument
The Algonquin vehemently contested the interpretation that the Crawford Purchase extinguished their rights along the Ottawa River. Their understanding, rooted in traditional territorial frameworks, was that Mississauga territory did not extend north to the Ottawa River. They argued that the boundary lay along the height of land, the watershed divide separating the St. Lawrence River system from the Ottawa River system. This perspective aligns with common Indigenous practices of defining territories based on natural geographic features like river basins and watersheds.
E. Justice Macauley’s 1839 Report
The boundary dispute became a subject of official review. In 1839, Justice J.B. Macauley was tasked with reporting on various Indian affairs matters, including the Algonquin claims. His report acknowledged the uncertainty surrounding the northern limit of the Crawford Purchase. As a potential resolution to the boundary question itself, Macauley proposed drawing a simplified line midway between the St. Lawrence and Ottawa Rivers. The very fact that such a compromise line was suggested implicitly confirms that the original 1783 agreement lacked the clarity needed to definitively place the boundary at the Ottawa River.
However, Macauley’s primary recommendation transcended the boundary issue. He concluded that the most expedient solution was the removal and relocation of the Algonquin people from the disputed region altogether. This indicates that, for the colonial administration, resolving the technicalities of the boundary was less important than clearing the land for settlement and resource exploitation.
The government’s deployment of the ambiguous Crawford Purchase against the Algonquin claim appears as a strategic maneuver. By leveraging the uncertainty inherent in the 1783 agreement with the Mississauga, colonial officials could create a legal pretext to ignore the specific claims and prior commitments made directly to the Algonquin and Nipissing. The boundary dispute, rather than being a genuine search for historical accuracy, served as a tool of legal obfuscation to facilitate colonial expansion and negate Algonquin rights in the Ottawa Valley.
VI. Post-Revocation: Government Reports and Continued Algonquin Marginalization
A. Macauley Report (1839) Implications
Justice Macauley’s 1839 report, while addressing the boundary dispute, ultimately pointed towards a policy of removal rather than accommodation. His recommendation to relocate the Algonquin signaled a clear governmental preference for extinguishing Indigenous presence in areas desired for settlement and industry. Tellingly, Macauley suggested that the costs associated with this relocation, potentially including annuities for the lost lands, should be funded through charges levied on timber licenses. This proposal explicitly connected Algonquin displacement to the economic imperatives of the burgeoning timber trade, acknowledging, albeit indirectly, that the industry driving the conflict should bear some responsibility for its consequences.
B. Bagot Commission (1842-1844)
Further shaping Indian policy in the Province of Canada (formed by the union of Upper and Lower Canada in 1841) was the Commission appointed in 1842 under Governor Sir Charles Bagot. Tasked with a comprehensive inquiry into the management of Indian Affairs, the commission, led by Rawson W. Rawson, John Davidson, and William Hepburn, examined the condition of various Indigenous groups, including Algonquins residing in Canada East (formerly Lower Canada) and other “unsettled Tribes”.
The Bagot Commission’s report, submitted in 1844, drew a distinction between the perceived land status in the two formerly separate provinces. It noted that in Canada West (formerly Upper Canada), Indigenous peoples were generally considered the original occupants, necessitating formal land cession treaties as settlement progressed. In contrast, the commissioners suggested that in Canada East, prior French land grants to Indigenous communities (like those at Sillery or Oka/Kanesatake) had effectively confined their territorial rights to specific, defined tracts. This perspective could have influenced how officials viewed claims, like those of the Algonquin, that spanned the Ottawa River boundary. The Bagot report also endorsed Justice Macauley’s earlier findings, including his rejection of Indigenous claims to a “separate nationality” distinct from Crown subjects. While specific details regarding the 1835-36 L’Isle-aux-Allumettes negotiations may not be prominent in the main report , its overall framework reinforced a move towards greater government control and assimilationist policies.
C. Pennefather Commission (1856-1858)
Building upon the Bagot Commission’s work, the Special Commissioners to Investigate Indian Affairs in Canada (often called the Pennefather Commission after Richard T. Pennefather) conducted further inquiries between 1856 and 1858. Their report reflected an intensification of assimilationist goals. They recommended consolidating scattered Indigenous bands onto larger, centralized reserves (such as Manitoulin Island, Garden River, Walpole Island) to facilitate administration and agricultural instruction. Critically, the Pennefather Commission went further than Bagot, suggesting that if Indigenous groups refused to surrender their smaller reserves and relocate, the government should consider expropriating their lands, arguing that reserve enclaves could not be allowed to impede provincial development. This recommendation underscored the hardening colonial stance, prioritizing settlement and economic progress over established Indigenous land tenure.
D. Continued Algonquin Petitions and Marginalization
Despite the government’s shift towards policies of removal and assimilation, the Algonquin people continued to assert their rights and protest their treatment. An 1847 petition, for instance, vividly described the negative impacts of settlement and logging. The petitioners lamented the loss of their former independence and wealth (“In those days Father we were happy, rich and independent”), contrasting it with their current poverty caused by the destruction of forests and game. They complained of being “ill treated by these interlopers” who destroyed their small maize patches and sugar bushes, and noted their powerlessness: “We are too weak Father to defend ourselves and obliged to put up with these injuries and injustices”. They reiterated their claim as the rightful proprietors based on the Royal Proclamation and ancestral occupation.
Throughout the remainder of the 19th century, Algonquin communities in the Ottawa Valley faced ongoing dispossession and marginalization. Many who did not relocate to designated reserves like Golden Lake (Pikwakanagan) remained as “non-status” Indians, living dispersed throughout their traditional territory but lacking official recognition or protection from the Indian Department. Their attempts to maintain traditional subsistence practices were increasingly hampered by resource depletion and restrictive laws. The history of these communities, often overlooked in official records, has been painstakingly reconstructed through research utilizing archival documents and oral histories by firms such as Joan Holmes & Associates, often in the context of modern land claim negotiations.
The trajectory of government policy following the 1836 revocation reveals a decisive shift. Where Colborne’s administration offered at least ambiguous recognition and potential protection on existing lands , Head’s denial , Macauley’s relocation proposal , and the subsequent recommendations of the Bagot and Pennefather Commissions demonstrate a clear move towards strategies aimed at extinguishing Algonquin title, consolidating populations onto reserves, promoting agriculture, and ultimately assimilating Indigenous peoples into the dominant colonial society. This policy evolution sought to erase distinct Algonquin presence and claims across large swathes of the Ottawa Valley, paving the way for unimpeded settlement and resource extraction, despite persistent Algonquin resistance.

VII. Conclusion
A. Summary of Findings
The attempt by the Algonquin and Nipissing Nations in 1835-1836 to secure a reserve at L’Isle-aux-Allumettes and confirm their rights in the Ottawa Valley represents a poignant case study in the complexities and inequities of Indigenous-Crown relations in 19th-century Upper Canada. Grounded in immemorial occupation and the principles of the Royal Proclamation, the Algonquin presented a formal petition to Lieutenant-Governor Colborne in 1835, outlining their grievances concerning land loss and resource depletion, and seeking confirmation of their remaining territory, compensation, and protection. While Colborne’s administration offered an encouraging, albeit ambiguously worded, response indicating willingness to assist with settlement and protection , this understanding was swiftly overturned by his successor, Sir Francis Bond Head, in 1836. Head invoked the prior, and vaguely defined, Crawford Purchase of 1783 with the Mississauga Nation as justification for denying the Algonquin claim to lands adjacent to the island. Subsequent government reports, notably by Justice Macauley in 1839 and the Bagot Commission in the early 1840s, reinforced this denial and shifted policy towards the relocation and assimilation of the Algonquin people, rather than accommodation of their rights within their traditional territory.
B. Analysis of Failure
The failure of the 1835-1836 negotiation stemmed from a confluence of factors deeply embedded in the colonial context. Firstly, the absence of a formally ratified treaty resulting from the 1835 discussions left the arrangement precarious and dependent on the goodwill of individual administrators. The documented response from Colborne’s office lacked the specific, binding language necessary to guarantee title or reserve boundaries. Secondly, the change in leadership from Colborne to the politically embattled and partisan Sir Francis Bond Head proved critical. Head’s administration prioritized colonial expansion and loyalty politics over nuanced engagement with Indigenous claims. Thirdly, the inherent ambiguity surrounding the northern boundary of the 1783 Crawford Purchase provided a convenient, if questionable, legal pretext for Head’s administration to dismiss the Algonquin petition. Finally, and perhaps most fundamentally, the overriding pressures of settler expansion and the demands of the burgeoning timber industry created a political and economic environment where respecting Algonquin territorial integrity was seen as an impediment to progress.
C. Significance and Legacy
The events surrounding L’Isle-aux-Allumettes in 1835-1836 had lasting consequences for the Algonquin people. The denial of their claim and the subsequent policies of relocation and assimilation contributed significantly to their ongoing dispossession and marginalization within their Ottawa Valley homeland. The failure to secure a recognized land base exacerbated their vulnerability to encroachment and resource depletion, as lamented in their later petitions. This episode exemplifies broader patterns observed across Canada during this era: the erosion of Indigenous land rights through contested treaty interpretations, the prioritization of settlement and resource extraction over Crown obligations, the impact of shifting administrative personnel and policies, and the persistent efforts of Indigenous nations to assert their rights despite facing overwhelming colonial power. The legacy of this period continues to resonate in contemporary Algonquin land claims, such as the comprehensive claim by the Algonquins of Ontario, which seeks to finally address the unresolved issues stemming from the lack of treaties covering vast portions of their ancestral territory.
D. Final Considerations on Source Interpretation
This historical episode underscores the critical importance of engaging rigorously with diverse sources. The Algonquin petition of 1835 offers a direct voice articulating their perspective and claims. Government correspondence reveals internal deliberations and official justifications, but must be read critically, recognizing potential biases and administrative objectives. Scholarly analyses provide essential context and interpretation but should be assessed alongside the primary evidence. Government reports, such as Macauley’s or the Bagot Commission’s, reflect the policy thinking of the time but were often framed by assimilationist assumptions and colonial priorities. Understanding the nuances, ambiguities, and contradictions within and between these sources is crucial for reconstructing a more complete and just understanding of the past and its enduring impact on the present.

title: GenAI for Historians
text:
Who This Guide Is For
This guide is for historians — faculty and graduate students — who are curious about generative AI tools but have not yet used them, or who have tried them briefly and want to understand what they can realistically do for historical research. No technical background is assumed. You do not need to know how to code, and you do not need to spend any money to get started.

title: GenAI for Historians
text:
What This Guide Is Not
This is not an argument that you should use these tools. It is not a technology manifesto or a prediction about the future of the discipline. Some historians will find GenAI useful for specific research tasks. Others will try it and decide it does not suit their workflow. Both are reasonable outcomes.
The goal is simply to give you enough hands-on experience to make an informed judgement.

title: GenAI for Historians
text:
How It Is Organized
The guide moves from free, low-commitment starting points to more advanced (and more expensive) tools:

What Are LLMs? — A brief, non-technical explanation of how these tools work, what they are good at, and why they make mistakes. Understanding the basics helps you use them more effectively and spot their failures.
Getting Started — How to begin with freely available tools (ChatGPT, Google Gemini) today, with no account setup beyond an email address. Includes a first exercise you can try in ten minutes.
Research Tasks — Concrete examples of how historians are using GenAI for transcription, translation, literature discovery, and coding. Each section includes sample prompts you can adapt to your own work.
Deep Research Tools — Several products now offer automated literature review: they search the web, read sources, and produce structured reports with citations. These are powerful and impressive — and they are also where GenAI’s tendency to hallucinate becomes most dangerous. This section explains what they do well and how to avoid being misled.
Case Studies — Two projects built by a historian using these tools: re-OCR’ing and building a searchable knowledge graph of 8,128 Royal Society articles (1665–1869), and transcribing and visualizing an archival collection of Joseph Banks’ correspondence on the British leather trade. Neither required advanced programming skills.
LLMs and Teaching — Why prohibition is difficult to enforce, why AI detection tools are unreliable (and biased), and what actually works: process-based assessment, Google Docs version history, assignment design, oral verification, and the ownership model for courses that move beyond prohibition.
Copy Editing — Using GenAI to proofread and polish your own drafted prose — what it does well, where it over-corrects, and how to stay in control of your voice.
Going Further — When free tools hit their limits and paid subscriptions become worthwhile. An honest look at costs, capabilities, and what the more advanced tools (Claude Pro, ChatGPT Plus, Claude Code) actually offer.
Limitations — What these tools get wrong, how to recognize it, and what you should never trust them to do unsupervised. This section is as important as any of the others.

title: GenAI for Historians
section: A Note on Tools and Vendors
text:
A Note on Tools and Vendors
This guide mentions specific products by name (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) because it is impossible to give practical advice without doing so. Naming a tool is not an endorsement. The landscape is changing rapidly, and the specific products available when you read this may differ from those available when it was written. The underlying skills — writing effective prompts, verifying outputs, knowing when to trust and when to doubt — transfer across tools.

title: GenAI for Historians
text:
Getting Started
If you have fifteen minutes and want to try something right now, go to Getting Started.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Three years after ChatGPT’s release, the evidence is clear: most university students use GenAI tools regularly, regardless of course policies. Surveys across multiple countries and institutions consistently find usage rates above 80%. A 2025 study at Middlebury College found that over 80% of students used GenAI for coursework — one of the fastest technology adoption rates ever documented in higher education. A UK survey the same year put the figure at 92% of undergraduates using AI in some aspect of academic work.
This does not mean every student is using GenAI to cheat. Most use it for legitimate tasks — explaining concepts, brainstorming, grammar checking. But it does mean that prohibition, as a policy, exists in tension with the reality of student behaviour. Faculty who choose prohibition need to understand both why it is worth attempting and why it is difficult to enforce, so they can design their courses accordingly.
This section is addressed to faculty in all positions on the spectrum: those who want to prohibit GenAI use, those who want to permit it cautiously, and those still deciding. The goal is practical advice grounded in what we have learned since 2023.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Three years after ChatGPT’s release, the evidence is clear: most university students use GenAI tools regularly, regardless of course policies. Surveys across multiple countries and institutions consistently find usage rates above 80%. A 2025 study at Middlebury College found that over 80% of students used GenAI for coursework — one of the fastest technology adoption rates ever documented in higher education. A UK survey the same year put the figure at 92% of undergraduates using AI in some aspect of academic work.
This does not mean every student is using GenAI to cheat. Most use it for legitimate tasks — explaining concepts, brainstorming, grammar checking. But it does mean that prohibition, as a policy, exists in tension with the reality of student behaviour. Faculty who choose prohibition need to understand both why it is worth attempting and why it is difficult to enforce, so they can design their courses accordingly.
This section is addressed to faculty in all positions on the spectrum: those who want to prohibit GenAI use, those who want to permit it cautiously, and those still deciding. The goal is practical advice grounded in what we have learned since 2023.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Why Prohibition Is Difficult
Prohibition is a legitimate pedagogical choice, particularly in courses designed to develop foundational writing and research skills. But it faces several structural challenges:
Ubiquitous access. GenAI tools are free, require only a web browser, and are increasingly integrated into the software students already use — word processors, search engines, email clients, and phones. There is no way to prevent access outside the classroom, and increasingly little way to prevent it inside the classroom.
Unreliable detection. AI detection tools (Turnitin’s AI detector, GPTZero, Originality.ai, and others) do not work reliably enough to serve as evidence of misconduct. Independent testing has found false positive rates far higher than vendors claim, and the tools are systematically biased against non-native English speakers and neurodivergent students. A growing number of universities — including Vanderbilt, Johns Hopkins, UCLA, the University of Waterloo, Michigan State, and the University of Pittsburgh — have disabled Turnitin’s AI detection feature entirely. Several students have successfully sued or settled after false accusations based on AI detector output.
Evasion tools. A thriving industry of “humanizer” tools (Undetectable.ai, StealthWriter, StealthGPT, and many others) rewrites AI-generated text to evade detection software. These tools are cheap or free, easy to use, and specifically marketed to students. Turnitin announced “AI bypasser detection” in August 2025, but this creates an arms race that detection is unlikely to win. The existence of these tools means that even if detection software were accurate — which it is not — determined students could circumvent it.
Chilling effect. Surveys find that roughly half of students report being afraid to use AI in any capacity for fear of false accusations, even in courses where some use is permitted. This punishes cautious, honest students while doing little to deter deliberate misuse.
The fairness problem. This is perhaps the most corrosive consequence of unenforced prohibition, and it deserves frank acknowledgement. When a significant number of students use GenAI to polish their writing while others do not, the overall quality of submissions rises. Faculty may not consciously curve grades, but they calibrate expectations against what they see. A student who writes competently but with occasional passive voice, awkward transitions, or poorly structured paragraphs — the normal imperfections of developing writers — will appear weaker relative to a cohort whose prose has been quietly smoothed by an LLM. The honest student’s B+ becomes a B; the AI-polished student’s B+ becomes an A-.
Students notice this. They see roommates and friends submit AI-assisted work and receive high marks. The message is clear: the system rewards those who use GenAI and punishes those who do not, regardless of what the syllabus says. This breeds cynicism, erodes trust in the instructor, and creates pressure on honest students to start using the tools themselves — not because they want to, but because they feel they have no choice.
This dynamic is one of the strongest arguments for moving beyond prohibition where possible, or at least for coupling prohibition with assessment methods (oral exams, in-class writing, process documentation) that cannot be gamed in this way. If the grade reflects demonstrated understanding rather than prose quality alone, the advantage of AI-polished text largely disappears.
None of this means prohibition is impossible. It means that prohibition enforced primarily through detection is a losing strategy, and that unenforced prohibition creates its own injustices. Effective prohibition requires a different approach.

title: LLMs and Teaching
section: The Problem with AI Detection Tools
text:
The Problem with AI Detection Tools
This deserves its own section because faculty may be under the impression that detection tools are a viable safeguard. They are not — and at the University of Saskatchewan, no AI detection tool has been approved for use. The university’s Academic Integrity office explicitly warns that these tools are biased against non-native English writers, produce false positives and false negatives, and can be bypassed through simple techniques. It further advises that instructors should not submit student work to third-party detection tools without permission, as doing so may constitute copyright infringement. Students who believe their work has been submitted to an unapproved detection tool without consent are directed to report this to their Associate Dean.
This is not merely a matter of institutional policy. It means that AI detector output cannot serve as evidence in a misconduct proceeding at this university. Faculty who rely on detection tools to identify AI use are building on a foundation that will not hold up if challenged.

False Positives
AI detection tools work by measuring statistical features of text — perplexity (how surprising the word choices are) and burstiness (how much sentence length and structure vary). Human writing tends to be more variable; AI-generated text tends to be more uniform. But these are tendencies, not rules, and the overlap is large.
Independent research has documented:

False positive rates between 5% and 20% on general student submissions, far higher than the <1% vendors claim.
A Washington Post test that produced a 50% false positive rate on certain content using Turnitin’s detector.
Accuracy that drops to 80–85% when text has been lightly paraphrased or edited — which is how most students actually use GenAI.



Bias Against Non-Native English Speakers
This is the most serious problem. A widely cited 2023 study found that AI detectors misclassified over 61% of essays by non-native English speakers as AI-generated, while achieving near-perfect accuracy on native English speaker essays. A follow-up found that 97% of TOEFL essays were flagged by at least one detector.
The reason is structural: detectors penalize simpler vocabulary, shorter sentences, and more formulaic structures — precisely the features of competent second-language academic writing. This is not a bug that can be patched; it is inherent to how the technology works.
In 2025, multiple institutions reported cases of international students accused of AI cheating based on detector output that was later shown to be wrong. A 2025 study found that Black students were disproportionately accused, with 20% reporting false accusations compared to 7% of white students.


Bias Against Neurodivergent Students
Students on the autism spectrum often produce writing that is highly structured, literal, and consistent in tone — features that overlap with the statistical signatures detectors associate with AI. Students with ADHD who write in focused bursts can produce similar patterns. Documented cases include students falsely accused who had to disclose their diagnoses to clear their names.


The Institutional Response
The American Historical Association published its Guiding Principles for Artificial Intelligence in History Education in August 2025, reflecting a two-year process of consultation. The AHA emphasizes that GenAI “can produce material that appears polished and credible, but assessing their outputs demands critical skills that the models themselves can neither teach nor foster.” The framing is disciplinary rather than technological: the question is what historical training requires, not what detection tools can do.
A growing consensus among teaching and learning centres holds that detection-based enforcement is unsustainable. As Inside Higher Ed reported in December 2025: “You can’t AI-proof the classroom, experts say. Get creative instead.”

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Best Practices for Prohibition
If you choose to prohibit GenAI use in your courses — and there are good reasons to do so, especially in first-year writing courses — the following practices are more effective than relying on detection software.

In-Class Writing
The most straightforward safeguard is supervised writing. If a significant assessment takes place in the classroom, GenAI cannot be used. Options include:

In-class essay exams (open-book or closed-book).
Supervised writing sessions where students work on drafts during class time.
In-class primary source analysis exercises.

Limitations: In-class writing tests speed as well as understanding and cannot be the sole assessment method for courses with substantial research components. There is also a serious accessibility constraint: students registered with Access and Equity Services (AES) often require extended time, separate spaces, or alternative formats for timed assessments. AES does not currently have the resources to support every course if large numbers of instructors simultaneously shift to 100% in-class assessment. A department-wide move toward in-class writing would place unsustainable demands on accessibility services and risk disadvantaging precisely the students these services exist to support. In-class writing is a useful component of a broader assessment strategy, but it cannot be the whole strategy.


Process-Based Assessment
Rather than evaluating only the final product, require evidence of the writing process:

Staged submissions. Require an annotated outline, a rough draft, and a revised draft at intervals across the term. Each stage should show genuine evolution — new ideas appearing, arguments being restructured, evidence being added or reconsidered.
Research logs. Have students maintain logs of sources consulted, search strategies used, and how their argument developed. These are difficult to fabricate convincingly because they require the kind of specific, idiosyncratic detail that GenAI does not produce well.
Reflective annotations. Ask students to annotate their own drafts, explaining why they made specific argumentative choices, where they struggled, and what they would change.

Limitations: Process-based assessment raises the bar for misuse, but it does not eliminate it. A student using GenAI can generate an outline, then a rough draft, then a polished revision, submitting each stage on schedule — producing process artefacts that look indistinguishable from genuine incremental work. Research logs can be fabricated after the fact. Reflective annotations can be generated by the same tool that produced the draft. The fundamental problem is that current models are good enough to simulate process, not just product: they can produce plausible false starts, evolving arguments, and self-aware reflections on writing choices.
This does not make process-based assessment pointless. It still catches the least sophisticated misuse (a student who submits a polished final essay with no prior stages), and the combined effort of generating convincing process artefacts and a final paper through GenAI is substantially greater than simply submitting an AI-generated essay. But instructors should be aware that staged submissions alone are not proof of genuine engagement. The strongest safeguard remains an oral component — a conversation in which the student must explain, in their own words, how their argument developed and why they made the choices they did. A student who fabricated the process will struggle to discuss it convincingly.


Version History as a Writing Process Record
Some word processors maintain a timestamped record of every addition, deletion, and edit made to a document. Google Docs is the best-known example, and version history tracking is widely used in K–12 and at other universities as a way to observe the writing process. The idea is straightforward: if you can see how a document was written, you can distinguish between text that was composed incrementally (with pauses, false starts, and revisions) and text that was pasted in as a finished block.






Google Docs Is Not Approved at USask



At the University of Saskatchewan, Google Docs is not on the approved learning technology list. Faculty should not require students to compose coursework in Google Docs. If you want to use version history tracking, use Microsoft 365 (Word Online), which is fully supported and integrated, or check whether Canvas provides relevant tracking for your assignment type. See the warning below for why this matters in misconduct proceedings.


What version history reveals:

Typing patterns. Genuine student writing shows incremental word-by-word and sentence-by-sentence additions, with pauses, deletions, false starts, and revisions.
Copy-paste events. AI-assisted work often appears as large blocks of text pasted in whole, with minimal subsequent editing. This is visible in the revision history.
Time spent. Total editing time and the number of sessions are logged. A 3,000-word essay that appears fully formed in a single 20-minute session is a different object than one built over five sessions across two weeks.
Editing trajectory. You can see whether ideas evolved organically or appeared fully formed.

Browser extensions like Revision History and Draftback enhance this further by providing video replay of the writing process.






Check Your Institution’s Approved Software



Before adopting any version-history approach, verify that the tools you plan to use are approved by your institution’s IT services. At many universities, Google Docs is not an approved platform for coursework, and third-party browser extensions like Revision History are almost certainly not vetted or supported.
This matters most in the context of academic integrity. If you use version history evidence to support a misconduct finding and the student challenges the decision, your case may be undermined if the evidence was gathered through unapproved software. IT policies exist in part to ensure that institutional processes — including disciplinary ones — rest on defensible foundations.
Faculty routinely use unapproved tools for their own convenience, and in most contexts the risk is low. But a misconduct proceeding is not most contexts. If you plan to use version history as part of your assessment or integrity strategy, confirm with your IT department that the platform is approved for student use, and ensure the tool’s data handling complies with your institution’s privacy policies.
At the University of Saskatchewan, for example, Google Docs is not on the approved learning technology list, while Microsoft 365 is fully supported and integrated. Word’s built-in version history offers similar functionality within the approved ecosystem. Canvas, USask’s LMS, may also provide relevant tracking depending on how assignments are configured.


Limitations even with approved tools: A determined student could type AI-generated text character by character, though this is time-consuming enough to be largely self-defeating — or it was, until recently. An ecosystem of tools now exists to automate exactly this. Often marketed as “Auto Typers,” “Ghost Typers,” or “Humanizers,” these tools simulate keystroke-by-keystroke typing into browser-based editors so that AI-generated text appears to have been composed in real time, complete with realistic pauses and corrections. Popular examples currently available in the Chrome Web Store include Duey.ai Auto Typer, Undetectable AI Human Typer, and SynthTyper, alongside various free, open-source scripts on GitHub. A student using one of these tools would produce a version history indistinguishable from genuine incremental composition.
This does not make version history worthless — most students will not go to this length, and the approach remains useful as one component of a broader assessment strategy. But it does mean that version history, like detection software, should not be treated as dispositive evidence of academic integrity. Students who draft in another application and paste will be visible in the history, which itself becomes a point of conversation.
Implementation: If you adopt this approach with an approved tool, state in your syllabus that all written work must be composed in the specified platform. Explain that you may review the version history. This is transparent, does not rely on detection software, and provides a basis for conversation rather than accusation.


Assignment Design That Resists GenAI
A common suggestion is to assign analysis of obscure or unpublished primary sources that are unlikely to be in the LLM’s training data. The logic is that the model cannot analyse what it has not seen. This was plausible in 2023 and 2024, but current models are multimodal: a student can photograph or upload any document directly into the chat, and the model will produce a competent analysis regardless of whether the source appeared in its training data. The obscurity of the source no longer provides meaningful protection.
That said, some assignment designs do raise the bar, even if none are fully AI-proof:

Personal engagement with sources. Ask students to reflect on their experience navigating a finding aid, visiting an archive, or encountering a physical document. “Describe what surprised you about the condition of the original manuscript” cannot be answered by a tool that has never been in the room. This is the most resistant assignment type, because it requires embodied experience rather than textual analysis.
Course-specific content. Require engagement with specific in-class discussions, guest lectures, or seminar debates. “How does Thompson’s argument about the moral economy relate to the point raised in seminar about the Saskatchewan wheat pools?” ties the assignment to a conversation the student had to be present for. A student could summarise the readings with GenAI, but cannot reconstruct the specific exchange that happened in class.
In-class source analysis. Rather than assigning primary source analysis as take-home work, conduct it in class under supervised conditions. This combines the pedagogical value of close reading with the assurance that the work is the student’s own.

Instructors should be realistic about what assignment design can and cannot accomplish. It can make outsourcing more effortful and less rewarding. It cannot prevent a determined student from using GenAI on any take-home assignment. The most reliable safeguard remains assessing understanding directly, whether through in-class work, conversation, or (at the upper level) oral examination.


Oral Verification
A short oral conversation about a written assignment is the single most effective safeguard against work a student does not understand. This can take several forms:

Oral exam on a research paper (15–20 minutes). The student discusses their argument, evidence, and research process. The grade reflects demonstrated understanding, not performance anxiety. One model already in use: “If you do the work yourself and write your paper, this will be a low-stress discussion of your evidence and arguments.”
Brief follow-up conversations (5–10 minutes). After returning a graded assignment, meet individually with students to discuss their work. This need not be framed as verification; it can be framed as feedback.
In-class presentations. Students present their research to the class and field questions. This is standard seminar pedagogy and serves double duty as GenAI verification.

Resource implications: Oral verification is time-intensive. A 15-minute conversation with each student in a class of 30 requires 7.5 hours of faculty time. This is feasible for seminars and upper-level courses but challenging for large lecture sections without TA support.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Moving Beyond Prohibition
Some faculty will conclude that prohibition, while legitimate, is not the right approach for their courses — particularly at the upper-undergraduate or graduate level. The following practices allow GenAI use while maintaining rigorous standards.

The Ownership Model
The most effective framework centres on ownership: students are intellectually responsible for every word they submit, regardless of how it was produced. GenAI may be used for specific tasks (literature review, transcription, copy editing, testing ideas), but the student must be able to explain, defend, and contextualize every claim in their work.
Under this model:

The syllabus specifies what GenAI use is permitted and what is not.
Students disclose any GenAI use.
An oral component (exam, defence, follow-up conversation) verifies understanding.
Work that the student cannot defend does not receive credit.
Students are advised that completing the core writing themselves is the safest approach.

This shifts the enforcement mechanism from “did you use AI?” (which cannot be reliably determined) to “do you understand your own work?” (which can be tested directly through conversation).


Disclosure Requirements
Where GenAI use is permitted, require disclosure. This normalizes transparency and makes concealment — rather than use — the integrity violation.

State in the syllabus exactly what must be disclosed and how.
Provide examples: “I used ChatGPT to identify relevant secondary literature, then verified each citation in JSTOR” or “I used Claude to help debug a Python script for data cleaning.”
Distinguish between incidental use (spell-check, grammar tools) and substantive use (drafting, research, analysis).



AI as Object of Study
One approach that serves both pedagogy and AI literacy: make GenAI output something students analyse critically rather than something they produce with.

Fact-checking exercises. Have students ask an LLM to summarize a historical debate, then identify what it gets wrong, what perspectives it omits, and what sources it fabricates. This teaches source criticism applied to a new kind of text.
Comparative analysis. Students write their own analysis of a primary source, then generate an LLM analysis of the same source, then write a critical comparison. Where does the LLM miss nuance? Where does it hallucinate context? What does it do well?
Historiographic audit. Ask an LLM to produce a reading list on a topic the student knows well. Have them evaluate it: which sources are real? Which are fabricated? Which are accurately characterized? This is a powerful exercise in bibliographic literacy.

These assignments cannot be completed by GenAI because the task is to evaluate GenAI’s limitations. They teach the critical skills students need whether or not they use these tools in their own research.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
What We Know Works
After three years of institutional experimentation, a rough consensus is emerging:

Detection tools are not the answer. They produce too many false positives, are biased against vulnerable student populations, and are defeated by freely available evasion tools. Multiple institutions have disabled them. Do not build your integrity strategy around detection.
Assessment design is the answer. Assignments that require demonstrated understanding — through oral components, process documentation, in-class work, or engagement with specific and idiosyncratic sources — are more robust than any technological fix.
Clarity is essential. Students report confusion about what is permitted. Every syllabus should state the course’s GenAI policy explicitly, including what is allowed, what is prohibited, and what must be disclosed.
Instructor autonomy matters. Different courses have different goals. A first-year writing course and a fourth-year research seminar warrant different policies. Faculty should be supported in making the choice that serves their students and their pedagogy.
The conversation is ongoing. No policy adopted today will be the right policy in three years. Build in review mechanisms and be willing to adjust.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Further Reading

title: Case Studies
text:
The earlier sections of this guide describe individual tasks — transcription, translation, coding, copy editing. But the real power of GenAI tools emerges when you combine them into a sustained research workflow. The two projects described here were built by a historian, not a computer scientist, using the kinds of tools discussed in this guide. Neither required advanced programming skills at the outset. Both started with a straightforward question and grew as the tools made each next step feasible.
The point is not that every historian should build a knowledge graph. It is that these tools lower the barrier to computational research methods enough that projects which once required a dedicated digital humanities team can now be undertaken by a single researcher with a clear question and a willingness to learn.
