title: Case Studies
section: Case Study 1: The Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society (1665–1869)
text:
Case Study 1: The Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society (1665–1869)

The Question
The Philosophical Transactions is the world’s first and longest-running scientific journal, published continuously since 1665. Its 8,128 articles and 106,829 pages document over two centuries of scientific thought in Britain and beyond — from Newton’s optics to Darwin’s early papers, from earthquake reports to experiments on air pressure. The full run is digitized and available through JSTOR’s Early Journal Content on the Internet Archive. But how do you actually use 200 years of scientific literature as a historical source? You cannot read 107,000 pages. Keyword search misses vocabulary drift (“electrical fluid” for electricity, “natural philosophy” for science). And the connections between articles — the people, the places, the specimens, the ideas — are invisible in a flat text archive.


What Was Built
The project proceeded in stages, each one made possible by GenAI tools:
Stage 1: Re-OCR the entire collection. The existing digitizations had mediocre OCR, particularly for early volumes with long-s typography and Latin text. Using OLMoCR (an AI-powered OCR system) running on H100 GPUs at a university cluster, all 8,128 articles were re-processed. The result: 197 MB of clean, searchable text, with language detection (English, Latin, French) and table identification. Processing took roughly a day of cluster time.
Stage 2: Extract entities. Using an LLM (Gemini), the project extracted people, places, and scientific taxa (species names) mentioned across the entire corpus. The result: over 86,000 entity mentions — 36,824 people, 32,527 places, and 17,122 scientific taxa — linked back to the specific articles and pages where they appear.
Stage 3: Build a knowledge graph. The extracted entities were assembled into a network: nodes for people, places, taxa, and articles, with edges representing co-occurrence (who is mentioned alongside whom) and document membership (which entities appear in which article). The graph contains 94,601 nodes and over a million edges. A temporal layer links articles to years, decades, and centuries, enabling queries like “show me all articles about electricity published between 1740 and 1760.”
Stage 4: Create searchable embeddings. Every page of every article was converted into a numerical vector (an “embedding”) that captures its semantic content. This enables similarity search: you can ask for pages that are about a topic, not just pages that contain a specific word. The vector index holds 92,786 page embeddings.
Stage 5: Build a query interface. The knowledge graph and vector index were combined into a GraphRAG (Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation) system. A researcher types a natural language question. The system searches the graph for relevant entities and the vector index for semantically similar pages, retrieves the actual text of the most relevant articles, and uses an LLM to synthesize a response grounded in the primary sources, with full citations.
Stage 6: Publish the archive. All 8,128 articles were published as a browsable, searchable static website with downloadable Markdown and JSON files for every article.


What It Looks Like in Practice
A historian studying Joseph Banks’ influence on botanical exploration types:

What role did Joseph Banks play in directing botanical research through the Royal Society?

The system searches the entity graph for Banks, finds the 1,599 pages that mention him, retrieves the most relevant articles, and generates a synthesized response drawing on dozens of primary sources — with clickable citations to each article. The entire process takes about 20 seconds.
A historian of electricity types:

How did understandings of electricity change between 1700 and 1800?

The system finds articles discussing electrical phenomena across the century, accounts for vocabulary change (it knows that “electrical fluid,” “Leyden jar,” and “galvanism” are all relevant), and produces a narrative overview grounded in the actual articles, not in the LLM’s general knowledge.


The Tools Used








Task
Tool
Difficulty




OCR processing
OLMoCR on university GPU cluster
Moderate (required cluster access)


Entity extraction
Gemini API (LLM)
Low (write a prompt, run a script)


Knowledge graph
NetworkX (Python library)
Low-moderate (LLM wrote most of the code)


Vector embeddings
NVIDIA NV-Embed-v2
Moderate (requires GPU)


Query system
Python + Gemini API
Low (LLM assisted with code)


Static website
Jekyll + Pagefind
Low


Deployment
Modal.com (serverless)
Low-moderate



The historian who built this project is not a software engineer. Most of the Python code was written with LLM assistance — describing what was needed, reviewing what the LLM produced, testing it on the data, and iterating. The skills required were: a clear research question, the ability to describe data clearly, and the patience to verify that each stage worked correctly before moving to the next.


What It Made Possible
The project has already supported research on ecological constraints in early modern science, the economic botany of the British Empire, and the history of tanning materials — topics that would have required months of manual searching to investigate across a corpus this large. The GraphRAG system does not replace close reading. It tells the historian which articles to read closely, and it surfaces connections between articles that no individual reader would notice across 8,128 documents.
The entire archive is publicly accessible, and the query interface costs roughly three cents per query to operate.

title: Case Studies
section: Case Study 2: The Banks Archive — Transcribing and Visualizing an Archival Collection
text:
Case Study 2: The Banks Archive — Transcribing and Visualizing an Archival Collection

The Question
Sir Joseph Banks (1743–1820) was president of the Royal Society for over 40 years and one of the most connected figures in British science. His correspondence — thousands of letters spanning botany, exploration, colonial administration, and industrial development — is scattered across archives worldwide. One collection at the Sutro Library in San Francisco contains letters focused on a specific episode: the British leather trade crisis of the late eighteenth century, when oak bark shortages threatened the tanning industry and Banks mobilized his scientific network to find botanical alternatives from across the Empire.
The letters were digitized but not transcribed. Handwritten correspondence from the 1790s is not searchable. The question was: could GenAI tools turn a collection of page images into a searchable, queryable, visualizable research resource?


What Was Built
Stage 1: Image processing and transcription. The digitized letter images were preprocessed (rotation correction, contrast enhancement) and then transcribed using an LLM with vision capabilities. The model was given context about the collection — the era, the handwriting conventions, the subject matter — which significantly improved accuracy. The result: 181 transcribed documents, ready for analysis.
Stage 2: Entity extraction and knowledge graph. Using the same LLM-assisted approach as the Royal Society project, the transcriptions were processed to extract people, places, commodities, and institutions. These were loaded into a Neo4j knowledge graph with 3,200 nodes and 5,300 relationships. The graph captures who wrote to whom, what commodities were discussed, which institutions were involved, and when.
Stage 3: Enrichment from linked data. The knowledge graph was enriched by connecting its entities to external databases:

Wikidata: Biographical data for 76 identified correspondents (birth/death dates, occupations, institutional affiliations).
VIAF (Virtual International Authority File): 735 published works linked to people in the network.
GBIF (Global Biodiversity Information Facility): 215 botanical specimens collected by members of Banks’ network, with geographic coordinates.
Historical trade data: 129 records of British tannin imports from 1800–1844, showing the commercial impact of the botanical alternatives Banks’ network identified.

Stage 4: Interactive visualizations. Five interactive visualizations were generated from the knowledge graph:

A timeline-network showing the leather crisis unfolding from 1773 to 1810, with Banks’ correspondence network layered on top — revealing how scientific knowledge, commercial interests, and parliamentary reform intersected.
A correspondence network mapping the top 120 correspondents as a force-directed graph, showing the structure of Banks’ intellectual community.
A correspondence flow diagram (Sankey) showing who wrote to Banks and who Banks wrote to, organized by decade, revealing asymmetries in the network.
A topic-commodity-place flow showing what was discussed, what materials were mentioned, and where they came from, across decades.
A temporal network anchoring people to the years of their correspondence, showing how the network evolved over time.



What It Looks Like in Practice
A historian studying the relationship between science and commerce in the late eighteenth century can now:

Search the transcribed letters for any mention of a specific commodity (cutch, valonia, oak bark) and see every letter that discusses it, with dates and correspondents.
Visualize Banks’ network of correspondents on the leather question and identify who the key intermediaries were between scientific institutions and commercial interests.
Trace how knowledge about a specific tanning material (say, Indian cutch) moved from botanical collectors in Bengal through Banks’ correspondence to commercial adoption in London, with dates at every step.
Cross-reference the correspondence with actual import data to see whether Banks’ advocacy had measurable commercial effects.

None of this was possible before the letters were transcribed. The transcription alone — turning page images into searchable text — transformed the collection from an archival curiosity into a usable research resource.


The Tools Used








Task
Tool
Difficulty




Image preprocessing
Python (rotation, contrast)
Low (LLM wrote the scripts)


Transcription
LLM with vision (Gemini)
Low (upload images, provide context)


Entity extraction
LLM (Gemini API)
Low


Knowledge graph
Neo4j + Python
Moderate (LLM assisted with Cypher queries)


Linked data enrichment
Wikidata, VIAF, GBIF APIs
Low-moderate (LLM wrote API scripts)


Vector embeddings
Gemini embeddings (3072-dim)
Low


Visualizations
D3.js, vis.js (generated by Python scripts)
Moderate (LLM wrote visualization code)





What It Made Possible
The project turned an untranscribed archival collection into a research infrastructure. The visualizations revealed structural features of Banks’ network that are invisible in the letters themselves — particularly the role of intermediary figures who connected scientific, commercial, and governmental spheres. The trade data integration showed that the botanical alternatives Banks’ network identified did enter British commerce at scale, a finding that would have required painstaking manual cross-referencing without the knowledge graph.

title: Case Studies
section: What These Projects Have in Common
text:
What These Projects Have in Common
Both projects share a pattern that is worth highlighting:

The historian drove the research questions. The tools did not generate the questions; they made it feasible to pursue questions that would otherwise have been impractical.
The historian retained responsibility for interpretation. Transcription, entity extraction, code generation, data transformation — these are the tasks the tools handled most directly. Current models are increasingly capable of synthesis and interpretive work, but in these projects the analysis, the arguments, and the historical judgements remained with the researcher.
Each stage was built incrementally. Neither project was planned as a monolith. The historian started with a simple task (transcribe some letters; re-OCR some articles), verified the results, and then asked: “What can I do with this now?” Each stage suggested the next.
The LLM wrote most of the code. The researcher described what was needed, the LLM produced Python scripts, and the researcher tested and refined them. This is the coding workflow described in the Research Tasks section, applied at scale.
Verification was constant. Transcriptions were checked against originals. Extracted entities were reviewed. Code was tested on real data. The tools accelerated the work; they did not eliminate the need for critical judgement.
The results are open and reusable. Both projects produced publicly accessible resources — a searchable archive, interactive visualizations, downloadable data — that other researchers can build on.


What Was Actually Hard
It would be misleading to suggest these projects were effortless. The genuinely difficult parts were:

Getting access to compute resources for large-scale OCR and embedding generation (university cluster access, or paid cloud GPU time).
Data cleaning — historical data is messy, inconsistent, and full of edge cases that no tool handles automatically.
Verification at scale — checking thousands of transcriptions and entity extractions for accuracy is time-consuming even when the error rate is low.
Learning enough about the tools to describe what you want effectively. The LLM writes better code when you can describe your data clearly.

What was not hard, and would have been prohibitively hard five years ago: the transcription itself, the entity extraction, the code generation, the visualization, and the construction of a queryable knowledge graph. These are the tasks that GenAI tools have made accessible to historians working without dedicated technical support.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
Large language models (LLMs) are software systems trained on enormous quantities of text. When you type a prompt, the model generates a response by predicting what text is most likely to follow, drawing on patterns learned during training. The result often reads like it was written by a knowledgeable human — but the process behind it is fundamentally different from human thought.
Understanding this distinction matters. It explains both why these tools are useful and why they fail in characteristic ways.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
Large language models (LLMs) are software systems trained on enormous quantities of text. When you type a prompt, the model generates a response by predicting what text is most likely to follow, drawing on patterns learned during training. The result often reads like it was written by a knowledgeable human — but the process behind it is fundamentally different from human thought.
Understanding this distinction matters. It explains both why these tools are useful and why they fail in characteristic ways.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
How They Work (Without the Jargon)

Step one: learning from text
Imagine you had read every book, article, and webpage published in English (and many other languages) up to a certain date. You would develop a powerful sense of what kinds of sentences follow other sentences — what arguments historians tend to make about the Atlantic slave trade, what a colonial office list looks like, how a French land grant is typically structured.
An LLM has something like that statistical sense, but without understanding. It does not know that the Treaty of Paris was signed in 1763 the way you know it. It has learned that in texts about the Seven Years’ War, the phrase “Treaty of Paris” is very often followed by “1763.” This initial training — called pre-training — produces a “base model” that is good at predicting text but not particularly good at being helpful.


Step two: learning from feedback
The dramatic improvements in LLM performance since 2024 are not mainly the result of reading more text. They come from a second phase of training that uses reinforcement learning: the same family of techniques that taught AI systems to beat humans at Go and Atari video games. The core idea is simple: the model tries something, gets a signal about whether it worked, and adjusts.
In practice, this takes several forms:

Human feedback: People rate model responses (thumbs up, thumbs down, or choosing the better of two answers). The model learns to produce the kinds of responses humans prefer. This is often called RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback).
Automated feedback: In domains like coding or mathematics, the model can get direct feedback from the world. Did the code run? Did it pass the tests? Did the proof check out? This tight feedback loop is one reason LLMs have improved so rapidly at programming tasks.
AI-generated feedback: Models are increasingly trained using evaluations from other AI systems, scaling the feedback process far beyond what human reviewers alone could provide.

The leading AI labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta) disclose relatively little about exactly how they train their models. The specific mix of techniques, the volume of human feedback, and the details of the reinforcement learning process are closely guarded. What is clear from the outside is that the combination of massive text training plus extensive reinforcement learning produces models that behave very differently from the base models trained on text alone.


Why this matters: the “emergent properties” debate
As these models have scaled up, they have developed capabilities that were not explicitly programmed and that their creators did not always anticipate. Models trained to predict text turned out to be able to translate languages, write code, solve logic puzzles, and pass professional exams. Whether these abilities represent genuine “emergent properties” — qualitatively new capabilities that arise from scale — or are better explained as sophisticated pattern matching is one of the most active debates in AI research.1
For historians, the practical takeaway is this: dismissing these tools as mere “autocomplete on steroids” is no longer tenable, as Cameron Blevins has argued.2 The gap between what these models produce and what a knowledgeable human would write has narrowed considerably. But the question of whether the models understand what they are doing or are performing an extraordinarily convincing imitation remains genuinely unresolved. Understanding this uncertainty is essential for knowing when to trust them and when not to.
When the pattern is strong and well-represented in the training data, the model produces accurate, useful output. When the pattern is weak, ambiguous, or absent, the model can produce confident-sounding nonsense — and may not signal the difference.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
What They Are Good At
LLMs excel at tasks where pattern recognition is the core skill:

Transcription assistance: Recognizing words in difficult handwriting or degraded print. The best current models now achieve accuracy comparable to expert human typists on English-language handwriting — a breakthrough that Mark Humphries has called “the bitter lesson” for the specialized HTR community.3
Translation: Producing serviceable rough translations, particularly for well-represented languages (French, Spanish, German, Latin).
Summarization: Condensing long texts into shorter summaries (useful for triage, not for close reading).
Code generation: Writing scripts in Python, R, or other languages for data tasks — often faster and more reliably than a historian learning to code from scratch.
Editing and proofreading: Catching typos, grammar errors, and inconsistencies in your drafted text.
Reformatting and structuring: Converting data between formats, extracting structured information from unstructured text.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
Where They Struggle
LLMs have improved substantially since 2024, but they still fail in characteristic ways:

Factual accuracy: They hallucinate — generating plausible-sounding but false claims, including fabricated citations, invented dates, and non-existent sources. Hallucinations have become less frequent in the latest models, but this actually makes them more dangerous: when most output is accurate, it is easier to let your guard down and miss the one fabricated source that slips through.
Archival research: Even with web search, they cannot access unpublished sources, manuscript collections, or materials behind paywalls. They have no way to visit an archive.
Interpretation and argument: Current models are surprisingly capable at synthesis and interpretation — they can identify historiographic debates, weigh competing claims, and construct plausible arguments. But the responsibility for evaluating whether an interpretation is sound, whether the evidence is sufficient, and whether the argument holds up still rests with the historian. The models are getting better at the form and substance of argument, but they do not know your sources the way you do.
Knowing what they do not know: They do not reliably flag uncertainty. A fabricated citation may be presented with the same confidence as a real one, though some models (notably Claude) are better than others at acknowledging gaps.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
The Key Habit: Verify Everything
The single most important skill in using these tools is verification. Never trust an LLM’s output without checking it against a source you control. This applies especially to:

Citations and bibliographic references (check that the book or article actually exists)
Dates, names, and factual claims
Translations (compare against the original, or a second source)
Code (test it; does it actually do what you asked?)

If this sounds exhausting, it is worth noting that historians already verify sources as a matter of professional practice. The habit transfers directly. The difference is that with an LLM, you must verify the tool as well as the source.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
section: A Note on Training Data and Web Search
text:
A Note on Training Data and Web Search
LLMs are trained on text that existed up to a certain date (the “knowledge cutoff”). Most major models now include built-in web search (sometimes called retrieval-augmented generation, or RAG) that allows them to look up current information in real time. However, web search does not always activate: depending on how you phrase your prompt, the model may answer from its training data alone and give you outdated information without warning. If you need current facts, it is worth explicitly asking the model to search the web.
Even with web search, LLMs reflect the biases, gaps, and emphases of both their training data and the sources available online. For historians, this means:

They know more about topics that are well-represented in English-language digital text.
They know less about topics that are primarily documented in archives, rare books, or languages with less digital presence.
They can reproduce historiographic biases present in the literature they were trained on.
Web search helps with recent events and verifiable facts, but does not give the model access to paywalled databases, archival finding aids, or subscription journals.

None of this is disqualifying, but it means you should treat LLM output the way you would treat any secondary source: as a starting point that requires critical evaluation, not as an authority.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
